claude's response:

 Now I have the full picture. Here's my assessment:

  ---
  Yes, we are ready for Phase 12b. Here's why, and what the remaining work involves:

  What's Already Built (Foundations for 12b)

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                Foundation                 │  Status  │                                What It Provides for 12b                                 │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Wire Format (WIRE-FORMAT.md)              │ Complete │ Language-agnostic binary ABI -- any language that can read/write bytes can implement it │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Host Imports (state, log, metrics, clock) │ Complete │ 9 host functions with stable signatures -- every SDK just needs FFI bindings            │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Rust Wasm SDK (aeon-wasm-sdk)             │ Complete │ Reference implementation of the aeon_processor! pattern                                 │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Rust Native SDK (aeon-native-sdk)         │ Complete │ Reference implementation of C-ABI export_processor!                                     │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Python SDK (sdks/python/)                 │ Complete │ Already has Event/Output types, wire format serde, host bindings, 6 tests, setup.py     │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ AssemblyScript SDK                        │ Complete │ Working TypeScript-like implementation targeting Wasm                                   │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ CLI tooling (aeon new/build/validate)     │ Complete │ Scaffold, build, and validate for any runtime                                           │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Wasmtime host runtime (aeon-wasm)         │ Complete │ Loads any .wasm regardless of source language                                           │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Processor Registry + REST API             │ Complete │ Register/deploy any artifact type                                                       │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ PROCESSOR-GUIDE.md                        │ Complete │ 4 runtimes documented                                                                   │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ CONNECTOR-DEV-GUIDE.md                    │ Complete │ Trait contracts documented                                                              │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ PUBLISHING.md                             │ Complete │ crates.io/Docker Hub/GitHub strategy                                                    │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  What Phase 12b Actually Requires Per Language

  The wire format is the key insight -- every SDK is essentially the same work:

  1. Deserialize Event from the binary wire format (read UUID, timestamp, source, partition, metadata, payload)
  2. Serialize Outputs to the binary wire format (write count, destination, key, payload, headers)
  3. Host function bindings (state_get/put/delete, log_info/warn/error, clock_now_ms, metrics)
  4. Ergonomic wrapper (Event/Output classes, builder pattern)
  5. Build toolchain integration (compile to .wasm)
  6. Tests (round-trip wire format, host stub, sample processor)

  The Python SDK already demonstrates this pattern completely. Each new language follows the same structure.

  Per-Language Readiness Assessment

  ┌──────────┬─────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ Language │     Wasm Toolchain      │                     Effort                      │                            Notes                            │
  ├──────────┼─────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Python   │ componentize-py         │ Already done -- SDK exists in sdks/python/,     │ Wire format + tests + host bindings complete                │
  │          │                         │ needs packaging for PyPI                        │                                                             │
  ├──────────┼─────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Go       │ TinyGo (wasm32-wasi)    │ Medium                                          │ TinyGo compiles Go to Wasm; wire format is straightforward  │
  │          │                         │                                                 │ with encoding/binary                                        │
  ├──────────┼─────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Java     │ TeaVM or GraalWasm      │ Medium-High                                     │ TeaVM compiles Java bytecode to Wasm; or use GraalWasm for  │
  │          │                         │                                                 │ JVM-native loading                                          │
  ├──────────┼─────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ C#/.NET  │ NativeAOT-LLVM or       │ Medium                                          │ .NET 8+ has experimental Wasm AOT;                          │
  │          │ Blazor Wasm             │                                                 │ BinaryReader/BinaryWriter for wire format                   │
  ├──────────┼─────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ C/C++    │ Emscripten or wasi-sdk  │ Low                                             │ Most natural Wasm target; header-only SDK with raw pointer  │
  │          │                         │                                                 │ math                                                        │
  ├──────────┼─────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ PHP      │ Not mature for Wasm     │ High                                            │ php-wasm exists but experimental; may be better as a native │
  │          │                         │                                                 │  FFI client                                                 │
  └──────────┴─────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  What I'd Suggest for Execution Order

  1. Python  → Already done, just needs PyPI packaging + componentize-py build integration
  2. C/C++   → Lowest effort (Wasm is native to C), header-only SDK
  3. Go      → TinyGo is mature, large Go user base
  4. Java    → Significant enterprise demand
  5. C#/.NET → .NET Wasm AOT improving rapidly
  6. PHP     → Lowest priority, Wasm tooling least mature

  One Thing to Decide Before Starting

  The roadmap mentions componentize-py for Python -- that uses the Wasm Component Model (WIT-based). But our current runtime uses the Core Wasm ABI
  (alloc/dealloc/process exports). There are two paths:

  Path A: Stay with Core Wasm ABI (current approach)
  - Each SDK implements alloc/dealloc/process manually
  - Python SDK already does this
  - Simpler, works today, all languages can target wasm32-unknown-unknown

  Path B: Migrate to Component Model (WIT-based)
  - Define a .wit interface for the processor contract
  - Use language-specific Component Model toolchains (jco, componentize-py, wit-bindgen)
  - More ergonomic, but requires Wasmtime Component Model support in the host

  The current codebase uses Path A. I'd recommend staying with Path A for 12b since it works and all the foundations are proven, then consider Component
  Model migration as a future phase when the toolchains mature further.

  Want me to proceed with Phase 12b execution?

✻ Brewed for 56s


==============================================================================

 can we discuss in detail about both paths, w.r.t. different programming languages as discussed in the above? How different it will be in future, if we
choose path A now and if we have to move to path B in future? How does throughput and processor development different in terms of path A & path B? When it
comes to PHP, we thought about four different deployment models though and I understand WASM support is not so mature, as written in the above. Finally, I
would like to explore WASM alternatives too, to support processor development in different programming languages, as a longterm stable option. Be it Apache
 flink or Arroyo or other, most are developed for few languages that are labelled as for enterprise languages. I wish to take Aeon into wider audience,
once stable. Doing so, will enable developers to take advantage of Rust language-based Aeon while being able to comfortably configure pipelines by
developing processors in their chosen language, as is supported in Aeon. How do you think? let us discuss and plan this in overall context once again.


===============================================================================
claude's response:

 This is a foundational architectural decision. Let me lay out the full analysis.

  Path A vs Path B — Core Wasm ABI vs Component Model

  Path A: Core Wasm ABI (Current)

  The processor exports three C-style functions. The host and guest communicate through raw shared memory using our binary wire format.

  Guest exports:
    alloc(size: i32) -> i32
    dealloc(ptr: i32, size: i32)
    process(ptr: i32, len: i32) -> i32

  Host writes event bytes → guest memory
  Guest reads, processes, writes output bytes → guest memory
  Host reads output bytes

  How each language implements this:

  ┌──────────┬────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ Language │      Wasm Target       │    Wire Format Implementation     │           Allocator           │                  Complexity                  │
  ├──────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Rust     │ wasm32-unknown-unknown │ aeon-wasm-sdk macro handles it    │ Bump allocator (SDK)          │ Low (macro hides everything)                 │
  ├──────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ C/C++    │ wasi-sdk / Emscripten  │ struct + memcpy, pointer math     │ malloc/free or bump           │ Very Low (natural fit)                       │
  ├──────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Go       │ TinyGo wasm32          │ encoding/binary.LittleEndian      │ TinyGo GC (limited)           │ Medium (TinyGo has restrictions)             │
  ├──────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Python   │ componentize-py        │ struct.pack/unpack (already done) │ Python GC via componentize-py │ Medium-High (Python→Wasm toolchain immature) │
  ├──────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Java     │ TeaVM → Wasm           │ ByteBuffer.order(LITTLE_ENDIAN)   │ TeaVM GC                      │ Medium-High (TeaVM subset of Java)           │
  ├──────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ C#/.NET  │ NativeAOT-LLVM         │ BinaryReader/BinaryWriter         │ .NET GC via AOT               │ Medium (experimental AOT)                    │
  ├──────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ PHP      │ php-wasm               │ pack/unpack                       │ PHP runtime in Wasm           │ High (entire PHP runtime in Wasm ~10MB+)     │
  └──────────┴────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Strengths:
  - Simple, proven, works today
  - Any language that can emit wasm32-unknown-unknown works
  - No dependency on Component Model toolchain maturity
  - Minimal overhead — just function calls + memcpy
  - Wire format is 100% under our control

  Weaknesses:
  - Each SDK must manually implement wire format serialization
  - No type safety across the host/guest boundary (raw bytes)
  - Every SDK duplicates the same alloc/dealloc/process boilerplate
  - Host function bindings are manual FFI (extern "C")
  - Upgrading the wire format requires updating every SDK

  Path B: Wasm Component Model (WIT)

  The processor is defined as a WIT (Wasm Interface Type) component. Language-specific toolchains generate bindings automatically.

  // processor.wit
  package aeon:processor@0.1.0;

  interface types {
      record header {
          key: string,
          value: string,
      }

      record event {
          id: list<u8>,       // 16-byte UUID
          timestamp: s64,
          source: string,
          partition: u16,
          metadata: list<header>,
          payload: list<u8>,
      }

      record output {
          destination: string,
          key: option<list<u8>>,
          payload: list<u8>,
          headers: list<header>,
      }
  }

  interface state {
      get: func(key: list<u8>) -> option<list<u8>>;
      put: func(key: list<u8>, value: list<u8>);
      delete: func(key: list<u8>);
  }

  interface logging {
      info: func(msg: string);
      warn: func(msg: string);
      error: func(msg: string);
  }

  interface clock {
      now-ms: func() -> s64;
  }

  interface metrics {
      counter-inc: func(name: string, delta: s64);
      gauge-set: func(name: string, value: f64);
  }

  world processor {
      import state;
      import logging;
      import clock;
      import metrics;

      export process: func(event: event) -> list<output>;
  }

  How each language uses this:

  ┌───────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────┐
  │   Language    │            Toolchain            │              What Developer Writes              │   Binding Generation    │
  ├───────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┤
  │ Rust          │ cargo-component + wit-bindgen   │ fn process(event: Event) -> Vec<Output>         │ Auto-generated from WIT │
  ├───────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┤
  │ C/C++         │ wit-bindgen-c                   │ aeon_output_list_t process(aeon_event_t* event) │ Auto-generated header   │
  ├───────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┤
  │ Go            │ wit-bindgen-go (wasm-tools)     │ func Process(event Event) []Output              │ Auto-generated from WIT │
  ├───────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┤
  │ Python        │ componentize-py                 │ def process(event: Event) -> list[Output]       │ Auto-generated from WIT │
  ├───────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┤
  │ Java          │ jco + ComponentModel JVM bridge │ List<Output> process(Event event)               │ Auto-generated from WIT │
  ├───────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┤
  │ C#            │ componentize-dotnet             │ List<Output> Process(Event event)               │ Auto-generated from WIT │
  ├───────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┤
  │ JavaScript/TS │ jco                             │ export function process(event: Event): Output[] │ Auto-generated from WIT │
  └───────────────┴─────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────┘

  Strengths:
  - Zero manual wire format code — WIT toolchains handle serialization
  - Type safety — the contract is machine-verifiable
  - One definition, all languages — change the WIT, regenerate all bindings
  - Rich types — strings, lists, options, records cross the boundary natively
  - Industry standard — WASI, Fermyon Spin, Wasmtime, Fastly all use Component Model
  - Forward-compatible — WASI Preview 2 is built on Component Model

  Weaknesses:
  - Toolchain maturity varies — Rust and JS are solid; Go, Python, Java are earlier stage
  - Serialization overhead — the Canonical ABI (Component Model's wire format) is more complex than our hand-tuned binary format
  - Binary size increase — Component Model adapters add ~50-200KB to .wasm files
  - Wasmtime version dependency — requires Wasmtime Component Model support (available since Wasmtime 14+, we're on 29)
  - Less control — the Canonical ABI is fixed; we can't optimize it for our specific hot-path patterns

  Performance Comparison

  ┌──────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │          Metric          │       Path A (Core ABI)        │          Path B (Component Model)          │
  ├──────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Function call overhead   │ ~5-10ns (direct call)          │ ~50-100ns (Canonical ABI lifting/lowering) │
  ├──────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Event deserialization    │ ~100-200ns (our binary format) │ ~200-500ns (Canonical ABI)                 │
  ├──────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Output serialization     │ ~80-150ns (our binary format)  │ ~150-400ns (Canonical ABI)                 │
  ├──────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Total per-event overhead │ ~200-400ns                     │ ~400-1000ns                                │
  ├──────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Binary size (.wasm)      │ 1.4-5KB                        │ 50-200KB                                   │
  ├──────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Memory footprint         │ Minimal (bump allocator)       │ Higher (Canonical ABI buffers)             │
  └──────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  For Aeon's target of <100ns per-event pipeline overhead, Path A's ~200-400ns Wasm overhead is already the floor. Path B roughly doubles it. However, this
  overhead is still orders of magnitude below Redpanda I/O latency, so in practice both are "fast enough" for all but the most extreme in-memory scenarios.

  Migration Cost: A Now → B Later

  This is the critical question. How painful is the migration?

  What changes in the host (aeon-wasm crate):

  ┌────────────────┬──────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────┐
  │   Component    │       Path A (Current)       │          Path B (Future)           │               Migration               │
  ├────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Module loading │ wasmtime::Module             │ wasmtime::component::Component     │ Replace loader (~200 lines)           │
  ├────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Function calls │ instance.get_func("process") │ Type-safe bindgen! generated trait │ Replace invocation (~100 lines)       │
  ├────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Memory access  │ Manual memory.read/write     │ Automatic via Canonical ABI        │ Remove ~150 lines of wire format code │
  ├────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Host imports   │ Manual Linker::func_wrap     │ bindgen! generated trait impl      │ Restructure imports (~200 lines)      │
  ├────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Wire format    │ aeon-wasm/src/wire.rs        │ Deleted (Canonical ABI handles it) │ Delete ~300 lines                     │
  └────────────────┴──────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────┘

  Total host-side migration: ~500-700 lines changed in aeon-wasm. Isolated to one crate. The pipeline, connectors, engine — none of them know or care
  whether the processor is Core Wasm or Component Model.

  What changes for SDK authors:

  ┌────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  SDK   │                      Path A → Path B migration                       │
  ├────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Rust   │ Replace aeon_processor! with cargo-component + wit-bindgen. ~30 min. │
  ├────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ C/C++  │ Replace manual exports with wit-bindgen-c generated code. ~1 hour.   │
  ├────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Go     │ Replace manual TinyGo exports with wit-bindgen-go. ~1 hour.          │
  ├────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Python │ Replace manual struct.pack with componentize-py bindings. ~1 hour.   │
  ├────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Others │ Similar — replace manual wire format with generated bindings.        │
  └────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  What changes for processor developers (end users):

  The processor function signature stays nearly identical:

  # Path A (current)
  def my_processor(event: Event) -> list[Output]:
      return [Output("dest", event.payload)]
  register_processor(my_processor)

  # Path B (future)
  def process(event: Event) -> list[Output]:    # WIT-generated types
      return [Output("dest", event.payload)]
  # No registration needed — WIT export handles it

  Migration verdict: The migration from A to B is contained and manageable. The blast radius is limited to aeon-wasm (one crate) and the SDKs (thin
  wrappers). Processor developer code changes minimally. Starting with Path A does not create meaningful technical debt.

  The PHP Question — Four Runtime Models

  The PHP situation is unique because Wasm tooling for PHP is indeed immature. The four runtime models from the roadmap are:

  ┌──────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┐
  │      Model       │                    How It Works                    │               Performance                │            Maturity             │
  ├──────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 1. PHP-Wasm      │ Compile PHP interpreter to Wasm, run PHP code      │ Slowest (~10ms/event). Entire PHP        │ Experimental. php-wasm exists   │
  │                  │ inside Wasm sandbox                                │ runtime in Wasm (~10-20MB binary)        │ but limited stdlib              │
  ├──────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 2. Native FFI    │ PHP calls Aeon via FFI extension (C binding to     │ Good for throughput. PHP process         │ Mature. PHP FFI works well. Not │
  │                  │ libaeon). Processor runs as PHP process            │ lifecycle is the bottleneck              │  sandboxed                      │
  ├──────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 3. gRPC/HTTP     │ Aeon calls PHP process over gRPC/HTTP for each     │ Moderate. Network overhead per batch.    │ Mature. Standard PHP deployment │
  │ sidecar          │ batch. PHP runs as a normal web server             │ But batch amortization helps             │  model                          │
  ├──────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 4. Message queue │ PHP reads from sink topic, processes, writes to    │ Decoupled. PHP speed is independent.     │ Mature. Laravel/Symfony have    │
  │  bridge          │ another topic. Aeon handles transport only         │ Natural PHP deployment                   │ Kafka consumers                 │
  └──────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────┘

  My recommendation for PHP: Model 3 (gRPC/HTTP sidecar) or Model 4 (message queue bridge). These play to PHP's strengths (web server, queue consumer)
  rather than fighting Wasm immaturity. Model 3 can be formalized as a ProcessorProxy — a built-in processor type that forwards events to an HTTP endpoint.

  This ProcessorProxy pattern is also useful for any language without good Wasm support:

  # pipeline.yaml — PHP processor via HTTP proxy
  pipelines:
    - name: php-pipeline
      source:
        type: kafka
        topic: raw-events
      processor:
        type: http-proxy          # Built-in proxy processor
        endpoint: "http://php-app:8080/process"
        timeout_ms: 5000
        batch_size: 100           # Send 100 events per HTTP call
      sink:
        type: kafka
        topic: processed-events

  Beyond Wasm — Alternative Processor Runtimes

  You raise an important point about reaching a wider audience. Let me map the full landscape:

  Comparison with Existing Engines

  ┌──────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │          Engine          │          Language Support          │                            How They Do It                            │
  ├──────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Apache Flink             │ Java, Scala, Python (PyFlink), SQL │ JVM-native. Python via Py4J bridge (slow). SQL for simple transforms │
  ├──────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Apache Kafka Streams     │ Java only                          │ JVM library. No other languages                                      │
  ├──────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Arroyo                   │ Rust, SQL, Python (UDF)            │ Rust-native. SQL for common patterns. Python UDF via subprocess      │
  ├──────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Apache Beam              │ Java, Python, Go, TypeScript       │ Multi-runner. Language SDKs talk to runner via gRPC (Fn API)         │
  ├──────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ RisingWave               │ SQL, Python (UDF)                  │ PostgreSQL-compatible SQL. Python UDF via subprocess                 │
  ├──────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Benthos/Redpanda Connect │ Bloblang (DSL), WASM               │ Custom DSL for transforms. WASM for complex logic                    │
  ├──────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Fluvio                   │ Rust, WASM (any language)          │ SmartModules compiled to WASM                                        │
  └──────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Key insight: No engine supports all languages natively. They all use one of these strategies:

  1. JVM-only (Flink, Kafka Streams) — limits audience to Java/Scala
  2. SQL + UDF (Arroyo, RisingWave) — SQL covers 80% of use cases, UDF for the rest
  3. gRPC/subprocess (Beam, Flink PyFlink) — cross-language but adds latency
  4. Wasm (Fluvio, Redpanda Connect) — sandboxed, polyglot, growing ecosystem
  5. Custom DSL (Benthos/Bloblang) — simple but limited

  Aeon's Multi-Runtime Strategy

  I suggest Aeon should support four processor runtime tiers, ordered by performance:

  Tier 1: Rust Native (.so/.dll)     — 240ns/event, 4.2M/sec
           For: Maximum performance, trusted code
           Languages: Rust, C, C++

  Tier 2: Wasm Guest (.wasm)         — 1.1-1.2us/event, 820K-940K/sec
           For: Sandboxed, portable, polyglot
           Languages: Rust, C, C++, Go (TinyGo), AssemblyScript
           Future: Python (componentize-py), C# (NativeAOT), Java (TeaVM)

  Tier 3: Embedded Interpreter       — ~5-50us/event, 20K-200K/sec
           For: Scripting, rapid prototyping, simple transforms
           Languages: Lua (mlua), JavaScript (boa/quickjs), Python (RustPython)
           Sandboxed, no compilation step, edit-reload workflow

  Tier 4: HTTP/gRPC Proxy            — ~0.5-5ms/event (batched: much better)
           For: Any language, existing codebases, microservice patterns
           Languages: PHP, Ruby, Java (Spring), Python (FastAPI), Node.js, anything
           Batch amortization: 100 events/call → 50us/event effective

  Tier 3: Embedded Interpreters — The Missing Piece

  This is what makes Aeon accessible to a much wider audience without waiting for Wasm toolchains to mature:

  ┌────────────────────────┬────────────┬──────────────────────┬─────────────────┬─────────────────────────┬─────────────┐
  │      Interpreter       │   Crate    │       Language       │    Overhead     │        Sandboxed        │ Binary Size │
  ├────────────────────────┼────────────┼──────────────────────┼─────────────────┼─────────────────────────┼─────────────┤
  │ mlua                   │ mlua       │ Lua 5.4 / LuaJIT     │ ~1-5us/event    │ Yes (restricted stdlib) │ ~500KB      │
  ├────────────────────────┼────────────┼──────────────────────┼─────────────────┼─────────────────────────┼─────────────┤
  │ boa                    │ boa_engine │ JavaScript (ES2023)  │ ~10-50us/event  │ Yes (no Node APIs)      │ ~2MB        │
  ├────────────────────────┼────────────┼──────────────────────┼─────────────────┼─────────────────────────┼─────────────┤
  │ QuickJS (via rquickjs) │ rquickjs   │ JavaScript (ES2021)  │ ~5-20us/event   │ Yes (no filesystem)     │ ~500KB      │
  ├────────────────────────┼────────────┼──────────────────────┼─────────────────┼─────────────────────────┼─────────────┤
  │ RustPython             │ rustpython │ Python 3.x           │ ~20-100us/event │ Partial                 │ ~5MB        │
  ├────────────────────────┼────────────┼──────────────────────┼─────────────────┼─────────────────────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Rhai                   │ rhai       │ Rhai (Rust-like DSL) │ ~1-5us/event    │ Yes (no I/O)            │ ~200KB      │
  └────────────────────────┴────────────┴──────────────────────┴─────────────────┴─────────────────────────┴─────────────┘

  Lua (mlua) is particularly interesting:
  - LuaJIT achieves near-native performance for simple transforms
  - Lua is used as an embedded scripting language in Redis, Nginx, Neovim, game engines
  - Minimal footprint, easy to learn
  - Perfect for "I just need to filter/transform JSON fields"

  JavaScript (boa or QuickJS) opens the door to the largest developer population:
  - Developers can write processors in JavaScript without a Wasm compilation step
  - Familiar function process(event) { return [...] } pattern
  - No build toolchain required — just a .js file

  Architecture: How This All Fits Together

  Pipeline Config:
    processor:
      type: rust-native           # Tier 1
      artifact: enricher.so

    processor:
      type: wasm                  # Tier 2
      artifact: enricher.wasm

    processor:
      type: lua                   # Tier 3
      script: enricher.lua

    processor:
      type: javascript            # Tier 3
      script: enricher.js

    processor:
      type: http-proxy            # Tier 4
      endpoint: http://php-app:8080/process

  All four tiers use the same Processor trait internally:

  // All of these implement the same trait:
  pub trait Processor: Send + Sync {
      fn process(&self, event: Event) -> Result<Vec<Output>, AeonError>;
      fn process_batch(&self, events: Vec<Event>) -> Result<Vec<Output>, AeonError>;
  }

  // Tier 1: NativeProcessor (libloading)     — already built
  // Tier 2: WasmProcessor (wasmtime)         — already built
  // Tier 3: LuaProcessor (mlua)              — new
  // Tier 3: JsProcessor (boa/quickjs)        — new
  // Tier 4: HttpProxyProcessor (reqwest)     — new

  Proposed Phase 12b Execution Plan

  Given all of the above, here's how I'd structure Phase 12b:

  Phase 12b-1: Core Infrastructure (enables all tiers)
    ├── ProcessorType enum: Native, Wasm, Lua, JavaScript, HttpProxy
    ├── ProcessorProxy (Tier 4): HTTP/gRPC batch forwarding
    ├── CLI: aeon new --lang <lua|javascript|python|go|...>
    └── Acceptance: PHP/Ruby/Java processors work via HTTP proxy

  Phase 12b-2: Embedded Interpreters (Tier 3)
    ├── LuaProcessor (mlua crate, feature-gated)
    ├── JsProcessor (boa_engine or rquickjs, feature-gated)
    ├── Script hot-reload (watch file, reload on change)
    └── Acceptance: Lua + JS processors pass E2E tests

  Phase 12b-3: Additional Wasm SDKs (Tier 2)
    ├── Go SDK (TinyGo → wasm32)
    ├── C/C++ SDK (wasi-sdk, header-only)
    ├── Python SDK packaging (PyPI + componentize-py build)
    └── Acceptance: Go + C processors compile and run

  Phase 12b-4: Language SDK Packaging
    ├── Python: PyPI aeon-processor package
    ├── Go: github.com/aeon-rust/processor-sdk-go
    ├── C/C++: Header-only in sdks/c/
    ├── Java/C#: Evaluate TeaVM / NativeAOT maturity, implement if ready
    └── Documentation: per-language quickstart guides

  Long-Term Vision

  Developer reaches for Aeon because:

  1. Maximum performance (Rust/C native processors)
  2. Polyglot (write processors in any language)
  3. Multiple deployment models per language:
     - Wasm (sandboxed, portable, millisecond startup)
     - Embedded script (zero build step, edit-reload)
     - HTTP proxy (any language, any framework, any existing code)
     - Native (maximum throughput, compiled)
  4. Always the same pipeline infrastructure underneath
  5. Same observability, delivery guarantees, upgrade strategies regardless of processor runtime

  The key differentiator vs Flink/Arroyo/Kafka Streams: Aeon doesn't force you into one language paradigm. A team can have their Rust hot-path processor at
  Tier 1 running alongside a data scientist's Lua filter at Tier 3 and a legacy PHP enrichment service at Tier 4 — all in the same pipeline cluster, all
  with the same delivery guarantees.

  What are your thoughts on this four-tier approach? Should I proceed with the execution plan?

✻ Crunched for 1m 53s

=============================================================================
 how much will be latency if we choose webtransport streams as a baseline, for processor development? This can be load balanced. This can use HTTPS 3.0
over QUIC protocol. This will have persistent connection, so connection establishment/re-establishment related port number saturation and corresponding
delays will not be there. Likewise, is overhead of websockets too. I am sure, this can be used with http 3.0 client (that can handle requests
asynchronously & in a non-blocking way and ofcourse, optionally in synchronous way too if needed), that can be developed in any programming language.
co-routines can be used. how do you think? can we process a simple event in nano seconds?

=================================================================================
claude's response:

 WebTransport Streams as Processor Communication Channel — Deep Analysis

  Latency Breakdown: Can We Process in Nanoseconds?

  Short answer: No, not over any network protocol. But we can get remarkably close to the theoretical minimum, and with batching, the amortized per-event
  cost can approach sub-microsecond.

  Here's why, layer by layer:

  Fundamental Latency Floor (Same Machine)

  ┌─────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │              Layer              │ Minimum Latency │                  Notes                   │
  ├─────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Kernel syscall (send/recv)      │ ~1-2μs          │ sendmsg/recvmsg on Linux, unavoidable    │
  ├─────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ QUIC packet processing          │ ~2-5μs          │ Encryption (AES-128-GCM), header parsing │
  ├─────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ WebTransport framing            │ ~0.5-1μs        │ Stream multiplexing, frame headers       │
  ├─────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Userspace serialize/deserialize │ ~0.2-0.5μs      │ Wire format encode/decode                │
  ├─────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Total minimum RTT (loopback)    │ ~5-10μs         │ Same-machine, no network hop             │
  └─────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Compare With In-Process Approaches

  ┌──────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────┬───────────────┐
  │             Approach             │ Per-Event Latency │   Relative    │
  ├──────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────┼───────────────┤
  │ Rust native (in-process)         │ 240ns             │ 1x (baseline) │
  ├──────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────┼───────────────┤
  │ Wasm (in-process, wasmtime)      │ 1.1-1.2μs         │ ~5x           │
  ├──────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────┼───────────────┤
  │ WebTransport (same machine)      │ ~8-15μs           │ ~40-60x       │
  ├──────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────┼───────────────┤
  │ WebTransport (same rack, <0.1ms) │ ~15-30μs          │ ~60-125x      │
  ├──────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────┼───────────────┤
  │ WebTransport (cross-AZ, ~1ms)    │ ~1-2ms            │ ~5000x        │
  ├──────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────┼───────────────┤
  │ HTTP/1.1 proxy (same machine)    │ ~50-100μs         │ ~200-400x     │
  └──────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────┴───────────────┘

  The key insight: WebTransport is 5-10x faster than HTTP proxy, but still 40-60x slower than in-process Wasm for single-event processing.

  Where WebTransport Wins: Batch Amortization

  This is where your intuition is exactly right. With persistent QUIC streams and batching:

  Single event over WebTransport:
    Serialize 1 event  → 0.3μs
    Send (syscall)     → 2μs
    QUIC encrypt       → 3μs
    Network (loopback) → 1μs
    QUIC decrypt       → 3μs
    Deserialize        → 0.3μs
    Process            → 0.5μs  (user logic)
    Return path        → ~9μs
    ─────────────────────────────
    Total: ~19μs per event → 52,600 events/sec

  Batch of 1024 events over WebTransport:
    Serialize 1024     → 100μs
    Send (syscall)     → 3μs    (one syscall, not 1024!)
    QUIC encrypt       → 15μs   (one TLS record)
    Network (loopback) → 1μs
    QUIC decrypt       → 15μs
    Deserialize 1024   → 100μs
    Process 1024       → 500μs  (user logic)
    Return path        → ~130μs
    ─────────────────────────────
    Total: ~864μs for 1024 events
    Per-event: ~0.84μs → 1,190,000 events/sec

  With batching, WebTransport achieves ~1.2M events/sec per stream — comparable to Wasm throughput, and this is per-connection. Multiple streams multiply
  this linearly.

  WebTransport vs HTTP Proxy — Why WebTransport is Categorically Better

  ┌─────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┐
  │        Property         │          HTTP/1.1 Proxy           │    HTTP/2 Proxy     │         WebTransport/QUIC         │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Connection model        │ Request/response                  │ Multiplexed streams │ Persistent bidirectional streams  │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Head-of-line blocking   │ Per-connection                    │ Per-stream (TCP)    │ None (QUIC per-stream)            │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Connection setup        │ TCP+TLS per request or keep-alive │ 1 TCP+TLS           │ 1 QUIC handshake, 0-RTT reconnect │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Port saturation risk    │ High (ephemeral ports)            │ Low                 │ None (single UDP port)            │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Batch support           │ HTTP body chunking                │ Frames              │ Native stream framing             │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Backpressure            │ HTTP 429 / retry                  │ Flow control        │ QUIC flow control (per-stream)    │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Load balancing          │ L7 LB                             │ L7 LB               │ L4 LB (UDP) or L7                 │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Kernel bypass potential │ No                                │ No                  │ Yes (io_uring + AF_XDP)           │
  └─────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────┘

  WebTransport eliminates every pain point of HTTP proxy while keeping the universal language support.

  The Persistent Connection Advantage

  You called out the critical insight: persistent connections eliminate the dominant cost.

  HTTP Proxy per-batch overhead:
    DNS lookup:        0 (cached)
    TCP connect:       0 (keep-alive)
    TLS handshake:     0 (session resumption)
    HTTP headers:      ~500 bytes overhead
    Connection mgmt:   Keep-alive timers, reconnect logic
    ─────────────────────────
    Effective overhead: ~5-10μs per batch

  WebTransport per-batch overhead:
    Stream open:       0 (reuse existing)
    QUIC framing:      ~20 bytes overhead
    Connection mgmt:   Built into QUIC (heartbeat, migration)
    ─────────────────────────
    Effective overhead: ~1-2μs per batch

  And critically — QUIC's connection migration means if a processor moves (container rescheduling, node failover), the connection survives without
  re-establishment.

  Architecture: WebTransport as Primary Cross-Language Protocol

  Here's how I'd revise the four-tier strategy to incorporate WebTransport:

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                  Aeon Processor Runtimes                 │
  ├─────────────┬──────────────┬────────────────────────────┤
  │   Tier 1    │   Tier 2     │        Tier 3              │
  │   Native    │   Wasm       │   WebTransport Stream      │
  │   (.so)     │   (.wasm)    │   (any language)            │
  ├─────────────┼──────────────┼────────────────────────────┤
  │  240ns/evt  │  1.1μs/evt   │  ~0.8μs/evt (batched)     │
  │  In-process │  In-process  │  Out-of-process            │
  │  Rust only  │  Rust/AS/C   │  Python/Go/Java/C#/PHP/... │
  │  Max perf   │  Sandboxed   │  Universal, load-balanced  │
  └─────────────┴──────────────┴────────────────────────────┘

  Tier 3 (WebTransport) replaces both the old "Embedded Interpreter" and "HTTP Proxy" tiers. Why?

  1. Better than embedded interpreters: No need to embed mlua/boa/RustPython in the Aeon binary. The processor runs as an independent process — its own
  memory, its own runtime, its own crash domain.
  2. Better than HTTP proxy: Lower latency, persistent streams, native backpressure, no port saturation.
  3. Better isolation than Wasm: Full OS-level process isolation. The processor can use any library, any system call, any GPU — no sandbox restrictions.
  4. Horizontally scalable: One Aeon pipeline can fan out to N WebTransport processor instances behind a load balancer.

  Language Support Reality Check

  HTTP/3 and QUIC client libraries exist for:

  ┌──────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┐
  │ Language │                 Library                 │            Maturity             │
  ├──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Go       │ quic-go                                 │ Production (used by Cloudflare) │
  ├──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Python   │ aioquic                                 │ Stable, async-native            │
  ├──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Java     │ Netty QUIC (netty-incubator-codec-quic) │ Incubating but functional       │
  ├──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ C#/.NET  │ System.Net.Quic (.NET 7+)               │ Built into runtime              │
  ├──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Node.js  │ @aspect-build/rules_js / experimental   │ Early but progressing           │
  ├──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ PHP      │ Via FFI to C libraries                  │ Indirect but feasible           │
  ├──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Rust     │ wtransport, quinn                       │ Production-ready                │
  ├──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ C/C++    │ ngtcp2, msquic                          │ Production-ready                │
  └──────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────┘

  For languages where HTTP/3 support is immature (PHP, older Node.js), we provide a fallback: the same wire format over plain WebSocket or HTTP/2 — just
  slower.

  Reaching 20M Events/sec With WebTransport Processors

  The math for your aggregate target:

  Per WebTransport stream (batched):     ~1.2M events/sec
  Per Aeon node (8 streams/partitions):  ~9.6M events/sec
  3-node cluster (with partition sharding): ~28.8M events/sec

  With kernel bypass (io_uring + AF_XDP on QUIC):
  Per stream:                            ~3-5M events/sec
  Per node (8 streams):                  ~24-40M events/sec
  Single node could hit 20M target.

  Even without kernel bypass optimizations, a 3-node Aeon cluster with WebTransport processors comfortably exceeds 20M events/sec aggregate — provided the
  processors themselves can keep up (which is their responsibility, not Aeon's).

  Protocol Design: Aeon WebTransport Processor Protocol (AWPP)

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │           Aeon Engine (QUIC Server)               │
  │                                                    │
  │  QUIC Listener (:4472)                            │
  │    ├── WebTransport Session (Processor A)          │
  │    │     ├── Bidirectional Stream 0 (control)      │
  │    │     │     → register, heartbeat, config       │
  │    │     ├── Bidirectional Stream 1 (partition 0)  │
  │    │     │     → batched events ↔ batched outputs  │
  │    │     ├── Bidirectional Stream 2 (partition 1)  │
  │    │     └── ...                                   │
  │    ├── WebTransport Session (Processor B)          │
  │    └── ...                                         │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  One QUIC connection per processor instance. One bidirectional stream per partition. This gives us:

  - Partition-level parallelism: Each stream processes its own partition independently
  - No head-of-line blocking: QUIC streams are independent — a slow partition doesn't block others
  - Natural backpressure: QUIC flow control per stream — if a processor is slow on partition 3, only partition 3 backs up
  - Same wire format: The existing binary ABI (from WIRE-FORMAT.md) works unchanged over WebTransport streams

  Implementation Phasing

  If we go this route, I'd propose:

  Phase 12b-WT1: Core Protocol (~1 week)
  - ProcessorTransport trait in aeon-types
  - WebTransportProcessorHost in aeon-engine (server side, using wtransport)
  - Wire format over bidirectional streams (reuse existing binary ABI)
  - Integration with pipeline's SPSC ring buffers

  Phase 12b-WT2: Reference SDKs (~2 weeks)
  - Python SDK (aioquic + wire format)
  - Go SDK (quic-go + wire format)
  - Node.js SDK (WebSocket fallback initially)

  Phase 12b-WT3: Production Hardening (~1 week)
  - 0-RTT reconnection
  - Processor health monitoring via control stream
  - Graceful drain (for processor upgrades)
  - Benchmarks: batched throughput, latency percentiles

  Phase 12b-WT4: Advanced (future)
  - io_uring QUIC for kernel bypass
  - Canary routing (send % of traffic to new processor version)
  - Multi-processor chaining over WebTransport

  Bottom Line

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                 Question                 │                                                  Answer                                                  │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Can we process in nanoseconds?           │ Not over any network protocol. ~0.8μs/event batched is the realistic floor, which is excellent.          │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Is WebTransport better than HTTP proxy?  │ Yes, categorically — 5-10x lower latency, persistent streams, no port saturation, native backpressure.   │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Does this replace Wasm?                  │ No — Wasm remains better for sandboxed, in-process, single-binary deployment. WebTransport is for        │
  │                                          │ languages that can't/don't compile to Wasm.                                                              │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Can we hit 20M events/sec?               │ Yes — 3-node cluster with batched WebTransport streams, even without kernel bypass.                      │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Should this be the primary               │ Yes — it's simpler, faster, and more universal than embedded interpreters or HTTP proxy.                 │
  │ cross-language protocol?                 │                                                                                                          │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  The revised tier strategy becomes:
  - Tier 1 — Native (Rust, max performance, in-process)
  - Tier 2 — Wasm (Rust/AS/C/C++, sandboxed, in-process)
  - Tier 3 — WebTransport (any language, out-of-process, load-balanceable)

  Three tiers instead of four. Cleaner, faster, more universal. What do you think?

✻ Cooked for 3m 45s

 As discussed in the above, if some of the programming languages are not fully ready, then, facilitating support of websockets (based upon HTTP 2.0) is

claude's response:

 Four-Tier Processor Runtime Architecture

  You're right — WebSocket over HTTP/2 as a fourth tier ensures no language is left behind. Here's the complete picture:

  Tier Overview

  ┌───────────────┬──────────────────────┬─────────────┬───────────────┬───────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │     Tier      │      Transport       │ Per-Event   │   Batched     │         Protocol          │                    Languages                    │
  │               │                      │   Latency   │  Throughput   │                           │                                                 │
  ├───────────────┼──────────────────────┼─────────────┼───────────────┼───────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ T1 — Native   │ In-process           │ 240ns       │ 4.2M evt/s    │ Direct function call      │ Rust, C, C++                                    │
  │               │ (.so/.dll)           │             │               │                           │                                                 │
  ├───────────────┼──────────────────────┼─────────────┼───────────────┼───────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ T2 — Wasm     │ In-process           │ 1.1-1.2μs   │ 820K-940K     │ Wasm ABI (WIRE-FORMAT.md) │ Rust, C, C++, AssemblyScript, Go (TinyGo), Zig, │
  │               │ (wasmtime)           │             │ evt/s         │                           │  Kotlin/Native                                  │
  ├───────────────┼──────────────────────┼─────────────┼───────────────┼───────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ T3 —          │ Out-of-process       │ ~5-15μs     │ ~1.2M evt/s   │ Binary wire format over   │ Go, Python, Java, C#/.NET, Rust, C/C++,         │
  │ WebTransport  │ (QUIC/HTTP3)         │             │ (batched)     │ bidirectional streams     │ Kotlin/JVM, Swift                               │
  ├───────────────┼──────────────────────┼─────────────┼───────────────┼───────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ T4 —          │ Out-of-process       │             │ ~400K evt/s   │ Binary wire format over   │ All languages — PHP, Ruby, Perl, R, Lua,        │
  │ WebSocket     │ (HTTP/2 or HTTP/1.1) │ ~30-80μs    │ (batched)     │ WebSocket frames          │ Erlang/Elixir, Haskell, Dart, Julia, Delphi,    │
  │               │                      │             │               │                           │ COBOL, and everything above                     │
  └───────────────┴──────────────────────┴─────────────┴───────────────┴───────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Language Support Matrix

  ┌────────────────────┬───────────┬──────────────┬─────────────────┬──────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┐
  │      Language      │ T1 Native │   T2 Wasm    │ T3 WebTransport │ T4 WebSocket │           Recommended Tier           │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Rust               │    yes    │     yes      │       yes       │     yes      │ T1 (max perf) or T2 (sandboxed)      │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ C                  │    yes    │     yes      │       yes       │     yes      │ T1 or T2                             │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ C++                │    yes    │     yes      │       yes       │     yes      │ T1 or T2                             │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ AssemblyScript     │    --     │     yes      │       --        │     yes      │ T2                                   │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ TypeScript/Node.js │    --     │   yes (AS)   │  experimental   │     yes      │ T2 (via AS) or T4                    │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Go                 │    --     │ yes (TinyGo) │       yes       │     yes      │ T3 (quic-go is production-grade)     │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Python             │    --     │      --      │       yes       │     yes      │ T3 (aioquic) or T4                   │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Java               │    --     │      --      │       yes       │     yes      │ T3 (Netty QUIC)                      │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Kotlin/JVM         │    --     │      --      │       yes       │     yes      │ T3 (shares Java ecosystem)           │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Kotlin/Native      │    --     │     yes      │       yes       │     yes      │ T2 or T3                             │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ C#/.NET            │    --     │      --      │       yes       │     yes      │ T3 (System.Net.Quic in .NET 7+)      │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Swift              │    --     │      --      │       yes       │     yes      │ T3 (Apple Network.framework)         │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Zig                │    --     │     yes      │       yes       │     yes      │ T2 (excellent Wasm target)           │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ PHP                │    --     │      --      │       --        │     yes      │ T4 (Ratchet, ReactPHP, Swoole)       │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Ruby               │    --     │      --      │       --        │     yes      │ T4 (faye-websocket, async-websocket) │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Perl               │    --     │      --      │       --        │     yes      │ T4 (Mojo::WebSocket)                 │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ R                  │    --     │      --      │       --        │     yes      │ T4 (websocket package)               │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Lua                │    --     │      --      │       --        │     yes      │ T4 (lua-websockets)                  │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Erlang/Elixir      │    --     │      --      │       --        │     yes      │ T4 (gun, Phoenix.Socket)             │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Haskell            │    --     │      --      │       --        │     yes      │ T4 (websockets package)              │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Dart               │    --     │      --      │       --        │     yes      │ T4 (web_socket_channel)              │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Julia              │    --     │      --      │       --        │     yes      │ T4 (HTTP.WebSockets)                 │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Delphi/Pascal      │    --     │      --      │       --        │     yes      │ T4                                   │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ COBOL (via FFI)    │    --     │      --      │       --        │     yes      │ T4 (via C bridge)                    │
  └────────────────────┴───────────┴──────────────┴─────────────────┴──────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────┘

  26 languages across 4 tiers. T4 WebSocket is the universal fallback — if a language has a network stack, it can be an Aeon processor.

  Interface-First Architecture

  This is the critical design principle. All four tiers implement the same logical interface — only the transport differs:

  /// Core trait — defines WHAT a processor does (aeon-types)
  pub trait Processor: Send + Sync {
      fn process(&self, event: Event) -> Result<Vec<Output>, AeonError>;
      fn process_batch(&self, events: Vec<Event>) -> Result<Vec<Output>, AeonError>;
  }

  /// Transport trait — defines HOW events reach the processor (aeon-types)
  pub trait ProcessorTransport: Send + Sync {
      /// Send a batch of events, receive a batch of outputs
      async fn call_batch(
          &self,
          events: Vec<Event>,
      ) -> Result<Vec<Output>, AeonError>;

      /// Health check
      async fn health(&self) -> Result<ProcessorHealth, AeonError>;

      /// Graceful shutdown
      async fn drain(&self) -> Result<(), AeonError>;
  }

  Every tier becomes a different implementation of the same two traits:

  ProcessorTransport
    ├── NativeTransport       (T1: direct fn call via libloading)
    ├── WasmTransport         (T2: wasmtime invoke, shared memory)
    ├── WebTransportTransport (T3: QUIC bidirectional streams)
    └── WebSocketTransport    (T4: WS frames over HTTP/2 or HTTP/1.1)

  The pipeline doesn't know or care which tier is behind it — it calls transport.call_batch(events) and gets outputs back. This is the interface-first
  guarantee.

  Wire Format Unification

  All four tiers use the same binary wire format from WIRE-FORMAT.md:

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    Aeon Wire Format                       │
  │                                                          │
  │  Event (host → processor):                               │
  │    [uuid:16][timestamp:8][source_len:4][source:N]        │
  │    [partition:4][meta_count:4]                            │
  │    [meta: (key_len:4, key:N, val_len:4, val:N) × count] │
  │    [payload_len:4][payload:N]                             │
  │                                                          │
  │  Batch framing (T3/T4 only):                             │
  │    [batch_id:8][event_count:4][events...][checksum:4]    │
  │                                                          │
  │  Output (processor → host):                              │
  │    [count:4]                                             │
  │    [dest_len:4][dest:N][key_len:4][key:N]               │
  │    [payload_len:4][payload:N]                             │
  │    [header_count:4][headers...]                           │
  │                                                          │
  │  T1/T2: shared memory buffer (alloc/dealloc/process)     │
  │  T3: WebTransport bidirectional stream                   │
  │  T4: WebSocket binary frames                             │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  For T3 and T4 (out-of-process), we add a thin batch framing header — batch_id for correlation, event_count for the receiver to know how many events
  follow, and a CRC32 checksum for integrity. The event/output encoding inside is identical across all tiers.

  Protocol Negotiation (Control Stream)

  When a T3/T4 processor connects, it establishes a control stream first:

  Processor → Aeon:
    {
      "protocol": "awpp/1",           // Aeon WebTransport Processor Protocol
      "transport": "webtransport",     // or "websocket"
      "name": "my-enricher",
      "version": "1.2.0",
      "capabilities": ["batch", "stateful"],
      "max_batch_size": 1024,
      "partitions": [0, 1, 2, 3]      // which partitions I can handle
    }

  Aeon → Processor:
    {
      "status": "accepted",
      "assigned_partitions": [0, 1],   // Aeon decides final assignment
      "batch_size": 512,               // negotiated
      "wire_format": "binary/v1",
      "heartbeat_interval_ms": 5000
    }

  Then one bidirectional stream per assigned partition opens for event/output flow.

  Performance Comparison (Summary)

  Throughput (single partition, batched):

  T1 Native  ████████████████████████████████████████  4,200,000 evt/s
  T2 Wasm    ████████                                    940,000 evt/s
  T3 WT/QUIC ████████████                              1,200,000 evt/s  *
  T4 WS      ████                                        400,000 evt/s  *

  * Out-of-process tiers scale linearly with processor instances
    T3 × 4 instances = ~4.8M evt/s (exceeds single-node native)
    T4 × 10 instances = ~4.0M evt/s (matches native via horizontal scale)

  The key insight: T3 and T4 trade per-instance latency for horizontal scalability. A single native processor is faster, but you can't scale it beyond one
  machine. WebTransport/WebSocket processors can scale to 100 instances behind a load balancer.

  Scaling to 20M Events/sec

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────┬───────────┬─────────┬─────────────────┬──────────────┐
  │             Deployment              │ T1 Native │ T2 Wasm │ T3 WebTransport │ T4 WebSocket │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────┼───────────┼─────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┤
  │ Single node, 8 partitions           │ 33.6M     │ 7.5M    │ 9.6M            │ 3.2M         │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────┼───────────┼─────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┤
  │ 3-node cluster                      │ 100M+     │ 22.5M   │ 28.8M           │ 9.6M         │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────┼───────────┼─────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┤
  │ 3-node + 4 processor instances each │ --        │ --      │ 57.6M           │ 19.2M        │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────┼───────────┼─────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────┤
  │ 3-node + 8 processor instances each │ --        │ --      │ 115M            │ 38.4M        │
  └─────────────────────────────────────┴───────────┴─────────┴─────────────────┴──────────────┘

  Even T4 WebSocket hits 20M with sufficient horizontal scaling (3 Aeon nodes, ~6 processor instances each). T3 WebTransport hits it with just 2 processor
  instances per node.

  Summary of the Four-Tier Architecture

  ┌────────────────┬───────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────┐
  │     Aspect     │         T1 Native         │           T2 Wasm           │      T3 WebTransport       │        T4 WebSocket        │
  ├────────────────┼───────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┤
  │ Deployment     │ .so/.dll linked into Aeon │ .wasm loaded into Aeon      │ Separate process, any host │ Separate process, any host │
  ├────────────────┼───────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┤
  │ Isolation      │ None (same process)       │ Wasm sandbox (memory, fuel) │ OS process isolation       │ OS process isolation       │
  ├────────────────┼───────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┤
  │ Hot-reload     │ dlclose/dlopen            │ Wasm re-instantiate         │ Reconnect stream           │ Reconnect socket           │
  ├────────────────┼───────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┤
  │ Debugging      │ GDB/lldb attach           │ Limited (Wasm traps)        │ Full native debugger       │ Full native debugger       │
  ├────────────────┼───────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┤
  │ Scaling model  │ Vertical only             │ Vertical only               │ Horizontal + vertical      │ Horizontal + vertical      │
  ├────────────────┼───────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┤
  │ Language count │ 3                         │ ~7                          │ ~10                        │ 26+ (universal)            │
  ├────────────────┼───────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┤
  │ Best for       │ Ultra-low latency         │ Sandboxed plugins, edge     │ Production polyglot        │ Maximum compatibility      │
  └────────────────┴───────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────┘

  Proposed Trait Hierarchy (Interface-First)

  // aeon-types/src/processor.rs — THE interface contract

  /// What a processor does (language-agnostic)
  pub trait Processor: Send + Sync {
      fn process(&self, event: Event) -> Result<Vec<Output>, AeonError>;
      fn process_batch(&self, events: Vec<Event>) -> Result<Vec<Output>, AeonError>;
  }

  /// How events reach a processor (transport-agnostic)
  pub trait ProcessorTransport: Send + Sync {
      async fn call_batch(&self, events: Vec<Event>) -> Result<Vec<Output>, AeonError>;
      async fn health(&self) -> Result<ProcessorHealth, AeonError>;
      async fn drain(&self) -> Result<(), AeonError>;
  }

  /// Lifecycle management
  pub trait ProcessorLifecycle: Send + Sync {
      async fn start(&mut self) -> Result<(), AeonError>;
      async fn stop(&mut self) -> Result<(), AeonError>;
      fn info(&self) -> ProcessorInfo;
  }

  /// Combined: a runnable processor with transport
  pub trait ManagedProcessor: ProcessorTransport + ProcessorLifecycle {}

  // Tier-specific config
  pub enum ProcessorRuntime {
      Native { path: PathBuf, sha256: String },
      Wasm { path: PathBuf, fuel_limit: Option<u64> },
      WebTransport { endpoint: SocketAddr, tls: TlsConfig },
      WebSocket { url: String, tls: Option<TlsConfig> },
  }

  The pipeline wires up Box<dyn ManagedProcessor> — it never knows or cares which tier is behind it. Source → SPSC → transport.call_batch() → SPSC → Sink.
  Same pipeline code for all four tiers.

  This is the cleanest expression of your interface-first principle: one trait, four transports, 26+ languages.

  Want me to proceed with implementing the trait definitions and transport abstractions in code, or do you want to refine any aspect of this architecture
  first?

✻ Cogitated for 1m 8s

claude's response:

Updated Four-Tier Overview

  Tier: T1 — Native
  Transport: In-process (.so/.dll)
  Per-Event Latency: 240ns
  Batched Throughput: 4.2M evt/s
  Throughput Scope: Per partition, single process. Vertical scaling only — bounded by single node CPU.
  Protocol: Direct function call
  Languages: Rust, C, C++
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  Tier: T2 — Wasm
  Transport: In-process (wasmtime)
  Per-Event Latency: 1.1-1.2μs
  Batched Throughput: 820K-940K evt/s
  Throughput Scope: Per partition, single process. Vertical scaling only — bounded by single node CPU + Wasm overhead.
  Protocol: Wasm ABI (WIRE-FORMAT.md)
  Languages: Rust, C, C++, AssemblyScript, Go (TinyGo), Zig, Kotlin/Native
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  Tier: T3 — WebTransport
  Transport: Out-of-process (QUIC/HTTP3)
  Per-Event Latency: ~5-15μs
  Batched Throughput: ~1.2M evt/s
  Throughput Scope: Per stream per instance. Horizontal scaling — N instances behind LB multiply linearly. 3 nodes × 4 instances = ~14.4M evt/s.
  Protocol: Binary wire format over bidirectional streams
  Languages: Go, Python, Java, C#/.NET, Rust, C/C++, Kotlin/JVM, Swift
  ────────────────────────────────────────
  Tier: T4 — WebSocket
  Transport: Out-of-process (HTTP/2 or HTTP/1.1)
  Per-Event Latency: ~30-80μs
  Batched Throughput: ~400K evt/s
  Throughput Scope: Per connection per instance. Horizontal scaling — N instances behind LB multiply linearly. 3 nodes × 8 instances = ~9.6M evt/s.
  Protocol: Binary wire format over WebSocket frames
  Languages: All languages — PHP, Ruby, Perl, R, Lua, Erlang/Elixir, Haskell, Dart, Julia, Delphi, and everything above

  Scaling Projection Table (with throughput scope)

  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┬─────────┬──────────────────────┬──────────────────────┐
  │              Deployment Topology               │           T1 Native           │ T2 Wasm │   T3 WebTransport    │     T4 WebSocket     │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┼─────────┼──────────────────────┼──────────────────────┤
  │ 1 partition, 1 instance                        │ 4.2M                          │ 940K    │ 1.2M                 │ 400K                 │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┼─────────┼──────────────────────┼──────────────────────┤
  │ 8 partitions, 1 node, 1 instance               │ 33.6M                         │ 7.5M    │ 9.6M                 │ 3.2M                 │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┼─────────┼──────────────────────┼──────────────────────┤
  │ 8 partitions, 1 node, 4 instances              │ — (in-process, no horizontal) │ —       │ 38.4M                │ 12.8M                │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┼─────────┼──────────────────────┼──────────────────────┤
  │ 8 partitions, 3-node cluster, 1 instance each  │ 100M+                         │ 22.5M   │ 28.8M                │ 9.6M                 │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┼─────────┼──────────────────────┼──────────────────────┤
  │ 8 partitions, 3-node cluster, 4 instances each │ —                             │ —       │ 115M                 │ 38.4M                │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┼─────────┼──────────────────────┼──────────────────────┤
  │ Target: 20M evt/s                              │ 1 node, 5 partitions          │ 3 nodes │ 3 nodes, 2 instances │ 3 nodes, 6 instances │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────┴─────────┴──────────────────────┴──────────────────────┘

  Key distinction:

  - T1/T2 — throughput is per-node ceiling. You scale by adding Aeon cluster nodes, each running its own in-process processor. Cannot add more processor
  instances independently.
  - T3/T4 — throughput is per-instance, independently scalable. Processor instances are separate processes/containers. You scale processors and Aeon nodes
  independently. This is the path to 20M+ with any language.
