============================
claude's response:

Good pivot — the memory-hierarchy question is exactly the right thing to nail down before any code. Let me lay it out so we're working from the same
  numbers.

  Where each tier actually lives

  ┌────────────────────┬───────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────┐
  │        Tier        │      Backing      │                                What the OS does                                 │     Crash behavior      │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┤
  │ L1 (DashMap)       │ Anonymous heap    │ Can swap under pressure (unpredictable)                                         │ Lost                    │
  │                    │ pages             │                                                                                 │                         │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┤
  │ L2 (mmap           │ File-backed pages │ Page cache IS the working set; OS flushes dirty pages on its own cadence        │ Lost pages = unfsynced  │
  │ append-only log)   │                   │ (dirty_expire_centisecs ≈ 30 s on Linux) or on fsync/msync                      │ tail (bounded)          │
  ├────────────────────┼───────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┤
  │ L3 (redb)          │ File + WAL,       │ fsync on commit                                                                 │ Durable up to last      │
  │                    │ explicit txn      │                                                                                 │ commit                  │
  └────────────────────┴───────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────┘

  Key insight: L2's "RAM" and "disk" are the same pages. A write is a memcpy into a page already mapped into our address space; the OS decides whether that
  page currently sits in physical RAM or is evicted to the backing file. We don't manage it — we just bound how much we care about.

  Pipeline-topology implications

  For a pipeline with 1+ sources, 1 processor, N sinks:

  - Event bodies need one storage path, not N. Write once to L2, fan out N sink-reads from the same mmap.
  - ACK cursors are per-sink-per-partition. N sinks → N cursors. These go in L3 (tiny — UUID + u64 offset).
  - GC of L2 segments = min(ack_cursor across all sinks). Slowest sink holds the log. This is where pipeline-level back-pressure kicks in if one sink
  stalls.
  - Per-source method: pull sources don't need L2 for replay (broker has it); push sources do (webhook/WS/WT have nothing to replay against).

  This means L2 is only mandatory for push sources when durability ≥ orderedBatch. Pull-source pipelines can skip the WAL and rely on source replay —
  cheaper.

  Concrete RAM/disk math (20M ev/s, 256 B avg)

  - Raw throughput: 5 GB/s of event bytes.
  - Retention window (time until all sinks ACK past a given seq): assume 30 s worst case = 150 GB of mmap on disk.
  - Resident RAM = whatever the OS keeps hot. For a streaming workload (sequential read after sequential write), the working set is small — recent segments
  in page cache, older ones evicted. Think ~2–8 GB resident on a machine with 32+ GB, OS eviction handles the rest.
  - fsync cost: amortized at batch/checkpoint cadence (1–10 Hz). Per-event mmap append ≈ 20–50 ns (memcpy + atomic offset bump). Within the 400 ns/event
  budget.

  Disk headroom is the real planning constraint, not RAM. User running at 20M ev/s with a slow sink needs to provision for the retention window ×
  throughput.

  Concrete proposal to react to

  - L2 holds event bodies, segment-rolled (e.g. 256 MB segments), append-only, mmap'd.
  - L3 holds per-partition {last_committed_seq, per_sink_ack_seq[N]} checkpoints, written at checkpoint cadence (not per-event).
  - GC: delete L2 segment S once min(per_sink_ack_seq) > max_seq_in(S).
  - Back-pressure trigger: L2 total size exceeds configured cap → same 3-phase PushBuffer protocol-level flow control you already have for push sources. For
   pull sources, just stop polling — broker queues up.
  - durability: none: skip L2 entirely. unorderedBatch/orderedBatch: L2 + checkpoint fsync. perevent: L2 + fsync every event.

  Questions for you

  1. Do event bodies go through L2 always, or only for push-source partitions? Pull-source pipelines have the broker as their L2 equivalent — duplicating to
   Aeon L2 is write amplification for no correctness gain. I'd vote: only push sources write to L2, pull sources track offsets in L3 and rely on Seekable
  replay.
  2. L2 size cap — global, per-pipeline, or per-partition? Pipeline scope matches your config model, but a single slow sink in one partition shouldn't stall
   the whole pipeline. I'd vote per-partition with a pipeline-total ceiling as a safety net.
  3. Should I pull up the prior dev work from git history (the L3-ACK-TTL thing) so we know what code already exists vs. what's new? I don't have it in
  context.

claude's response:

 Agreed on the ETL framing — that reinforces per-pipeline as the user-facing unit. Here's a concrete three-level budget that covers both the "one big
  pipeline" and "mixed pipelines" deployments:

  L2 budget hierarchy

  ┌───────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┐
  │       Level       │                     Purpose                      │                 Config                  │              Default              │
  ├───────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Node-global cap   │ Disk-size safety net; prevents a runaway         │ state.l2.node_max_bytes                 │ e.g. 80% of configured state dir  │
  │                   │ pipeline from eating all available storage       │                                         │ volume                            │
  ├───────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Per-pipeline cap  │ Fair-share ceiling when multiple pipelines       │ pipeline.durability.l2_max_bytes        │ unset → inherits node-global      │
  │                   │ coexist; defines each pipeline's SLO envelope    │                                         │ (single-pipeline case just works) │
  ├───────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Per-partition     │ Early-warning threshold so one hot partition     │ derived = pipeline_cap /                │                                   │
  │ soft target       │ backs off before it starves its siblings in the  │ partition_count, overridable            │ auto                              │
  │                   │ same pipeline                                    │                                         │                                   │
  └───────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────┘

  Backpressure escalation

  1. Partition exceeds soft target → slow that partition's source only (push: Phase 3 flow control; pull: stop polling, broker queues).
  2. Pipeline approaches its cap → throttle all partitions in that pipeline.
  3. Node approaches global cap → log warning, throttle all pipelines, emit metric aeon_l2_pressure{level="node"} for ops alerting.

  This is what makes Aeon feel like ETL rather than a single-tenant streaming engine: operators size pipelines independently, noisy pipelines can't take
  down quiet ones, and the single-big-pipeline case needs zero extra config (just don't set a pipeline cap).

  Let me capture the ETL framing in memory since it's a lens on the product, then two quick asks:

  Still pending from me:
  - Q3: want me to pull the prior L3-ACK-TTL dev work out of git history so we know what code already exists before designing the new WAL layer?
  - Would you also want L2 durability class to be configurable (fsync_per_event / fsync_per_batch / fsync_on_checkpoint) orthogonal to the cap? That's where
   the perevent / orderedBatch / unorderedBatch knob lives.

  Recalled 1 memory, wrote 2 memories (ctrl+o to expand)

claude's response:

Agreed — Redpanda was your dev-testing choice, not a design anchor. Let me audit what's actually in the tree before we talk design.

 Audit done. Key finding: Aeon today has metadata/offset durability but zero event-body persistence. Everything we've been discussing as "new" is genuinely
   greenfield for event bodies — but the scaffolding around it is mostly in place.

  What already exists (don't rebuild)

  ┌─────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┐
  │            Piece            │                 File                 │                   Role                    │               Notes                │
  ├─────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                             │                                      │ In-memory per-event state                 │                                    │
  │ DeliveryLedger              │ aeon-engine/src/delivery_ledger.rs   │ (pending/failed, retry counts,            │ IDs + offsets only, no payloads    │
  │                             │                                      │ checkpoint_offsets())                     │                                    │
  ├─────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ CheckpointRecord +          │                                      │ Append-only WAL: monotonic ID,            │                                    │
  │ CheckpointWriter            │ aeon-engine/src/checkpoint.rs        │ per-partition offsets, pending_event_ids: │ Metadata, ~100–200 bytes/record    │
  │                             │                                      │  Vec<[u8; 16]>, CRC32                     │                                    │
  ├─────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ L3CheckpointStore (FT-3)    │ same file                            │ L3-backed alternative to WAL file         │ checkpoint/{id} keys, scan-prefix  │
  │                             │                                      │                                           │ ordered                            │
  ├─────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                             │                                      │                                           │ Already matches your naming — but  │
  │ DeliveryStrategy            │ aeon-types/src/delivery.rs           │ PerEvent / OrderedBatch / UnorderedBatch  │ controls ack timing, not           │
  │                             │                                      │                                           │ persistence                        │
  ├─────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ DeliverySemantics           │ same                                 │ AtLeastOnce / ExactlyOnce                 │ Pipeline-config surface exists     │
  ├─────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                             │                                      │                                           │ Kafka-as-checkpoint is a shipped   │
  │ CheckpointConfig.backend    │ same                                 │ Wal / StateStore / Kafka / None           │ option — reinforces the bias you   │
  │                             │                                      │                                           │ flagged                            │
  ├─────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ FlushStrategy               │ same                                 │ interval, max_pending, adaptive knobs     │ 1s / 50K default                   │
  ├─────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ L2 MmapStore                │ aeon-state/src/l2.rs                 │ Append-only mmap log with index +         │ Exists, feature-gated, unused for  │
  │                             │                                      │ compaction                                │ events                             │
  ├─────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ BatchInflight /             │ aeon-engine/src/transport/session.rs │ Transient RAM retention for T3/T4         │ Payloads in RAM only, no disk      │
  │ ReplayOrchestrator          │                                      │ disconnect (~30s)                         │ backing                            │
  └─────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────┘

  What this changes about the design

  1. The perevent / orderedBatch / unorderedBatch config knob is already there — we extend its meaning from "ack timing" to also cover "event-body
  persistence tier." No new config surface, just richer semantics.
  2. CheckpointConfig has Kafka as a backend option. That's where the Kafka-as-default creep you called out leaked into the code. A clean design should
  deprecate CheckpointBackend::Kafka (or at least demote it from peer status) in favor of Wal / StateStore as the only Aeon-owned options — Kafka as a
  checkpoint store is exactly the coupling you're objecting to.
  3. L2 MmapStore is 646 lines of shipped, tested code sitting idle for this use case. It's already mmap-backed, append-only, has recovery + compaction. The
   event-body WAL we sketched is this file with a thin pipeline-runner integration, per-partition segments, and GC keyed off min(ack_cursor).
  4. BatchInflight/ReplayOrchestrator solve a narrower version of this problem (processor-disconnect only) by keeping payloads in RAM. Once L2-backed event
  persistence lands, the replay orchestrator becomes a consumer of L2 rather than its own RAM retention scheme — simplification.

  Questions before we freeze the shape

  1. Deprecate CheckpointBackend::Kafka? I'd say yes — it's the single strongest symbol of the Kafka-default bias. Anyone who actually wants it can emit
  checkpoints to a Kafka sink externally.
  2. Should L2 carry both event bodies and checkpoint records, or keep them split (L2 for bodies, L3/redb for checkpoints as today)? Splitting matches the
  current code; unifying means one tier to GC. I'd keep split — checkpoints are transactional, bodies are append-only, different access patterns.
  3. BatchInflight/ReplayOrchestrator — refactor as L2 readers later, or leave them as a fast-path RAM cache on top of L2? I'd leave them as a RAM cache
  (tier above L2) so T3/T4 recovery stays sub-ms when there's been no crash.

  No code yet. Ball to you on those three plus whether my read of "extend existing mode enum rather than add a new one" matches your intent.

  cooperation. Next: you decide whether to deprecate CheckpointBackend::Kafka and confirm keeping event bodies in L2 separate from L3 checkpoints. (disable
  recaps in /config)

claude's response:

 Confirmed in code. Important catches from reading the actual files:

  On (1) deprecating CheckpointBackend::Kafka: pipeline.rs:873 already treats Kafka | None as "no persister" — so it's currently a no-op enum variant that
  looks like a feature. Removal is a rename + test/bench cleanup, no behavioral loss.

  On (3) WAL as L3 fallback — that is not how the code works today. Wal and StateStore are peers in delivery.rs:60 + pipeline.rs:834-873; picking StateStore
   without an l3_checkpoint_store just logs a warning and silently persists nothing. So this is a new design constraint to encode, not an existing pattern.
  The shape it should take:
  - backend: StateStore → try L3 first; on L3 open/write error, automatically failover to a WAL file in the state dir, log the degradation, emit a metric.
  - backend: Wal stays available as an explicit "I want WAL, no L3" choice.
  - backend: None stays as the durability-off escape hatch.
  - backend: Kafka → removed.

  On (4) BatchInflight/ReplayOrchestrator uniformity — one subtlety worth your input: the uniformity gain only exists when durability ≥ unorderedBatch
  (events are in L2 anyway, so BatchInflight becomes a cursor map instead of an event holder). For durability: none, there's no L2 to read from — we either:
  - (a) keep today's RAM-retention orchestrator as the fallback when durability is off (dual-mode code), or
  - (b) say "no durability = no processor-disconnect replay either" — honest coupling, drop the RAM retention, T3/T4 reconnect = accept the in-flight loss.

  (b) is ideologically cleaner and removes code; (a) preserves the UX of today's replay window even for users who don't want full durability. Which
  direction do you want?

  Frozen so far

  - Source owns ingest UUIDv7, pull/push, Seekable.
  - Sink owns EOS tier + native primitive (T1–T6 matrix).
  - No intermediate pipeline topic.
  - L2 mmap = event body spine (push-source partitions only; pull sources rely on broker + Seekable).
  - L3 (redb/rocksdb/pluggable) = checkpoint records; WAL is automatic fallback of L3, not a peer.
  - CheckpointBackend::Kafka → removed.
  - Budget hierarchy: node-global cap > per-pipeline cap > per-partition soft target.
  - Durability mode names reuse existing DeliveryStrategy enum: PerEvent / OrderedBatch / UnorderedBatch + None. Semantics extended to cover event-body
  persistence, not just ack timing.
  - Per-pipeline config surface (ETL framing: noisy pipelines can't starve quiet ones).

  Still open

  - Q4 shape above (BatchInflight uniformity: option (a) or (b)).
  - Push-source ingest ack boundary from earlier: when a webhook POST lands, do we return 2xx after L2 append (correctness) or before (throughput)? PerEvent
   pins this to "after." What's the default for OrderedBatch / UnorderedBatch?
  - None mode behavior for push sources: today's PushBuffer keeps events in RAM until consumed. No change needed, but want to confirm that's the intent vs.
  rejecting push sources entirely when durability: none.

claude's response:

Good catches on both.

  Q1: UUIDv7 for pull-source events

  Right — if pull events don't hit L2, we need an identity story that survives replay. Three options:

  - (A) Deterministic UUIDv7: minted at runtime from (source_name, partition, broker_offset) — hash supplies the random bits, source_ts supplies the 48-bit
  timestamp prefix. Property: replay after crash produces the same UUID for the same logical event, so sink-side idempotency-key dedup (T4/T5) keeps working
   without persisting anything. No storage cost.
  - (B) Random UUIDv7 + persist mapping: mint normally, write (uuid → broker_offset) to L2 before sink. Defeats the "pull sources don't write L2" rule.
  - (C) Drop UUIDv7 for pull sources: sink dedup falls back to broker-native identity (offset + partition). Asymmetric abstraction — sinks need two dedup
  paths.

  (A) is the right call. It preserves "one event identity" across source types, costs zero storage, and works for any source with a stable replay position
  (Kafka offset, Postgres LSN, MySQL binlog pos, Mongo resume token, file byte offset). Push sources mint the standard random UUIDv7 since there's no broker
   position to hash — and for them the event body hits L2 anyway, so replay reads the stored UUID.

  HTTP-polling source is the one awkward case (no strict offset). Treat it like push for identity purposes: mint random, write to L2 when durability is on.

  Q2: BatchInflight — 4(a) with explicit "best-effort" labeling

  Frozen: keep the RAM-only ReplayOrchestrator active regardless of durability mode, but label it in code + docs as a graceful convenience for transient
  processor disconnects only — not a durability guarantee. Crash loses events in the replay window; that's by design when durability: none. Metric + log
  message on activation make the best-effort nature visible to operators.

  Updated frozen list

  - Source owns event identity: push sources mint random UUIDv7; pull sources mint deterministic UUIDv7 from hash(source, partition, offset) + source_ts;
  both at runtime, no persistence required for pull.
  - CheckpointBackend::Kafka removed; WAL is automatic fallback of L3 StateStore, not a peer.
  - L2 = event bodies (push + HTTP-polling only); L3 = checkpoint records.
  - ReplayOrchestrator stays RAM-only across all durability modes, documented as best-effort.
  - Budget hierarchy + per-pipeline config per ETL framing.
  - Durability modes reuse DeliveryStrategy enum (PerEvent / OrderedBatch / UnorderedBatch / None), semantics extended to cover event-body persistence.

  Still open

  1. Push-source ack boundary: webhook/WS/WT acking 2xx/pong before vs after L2 append. PerEvent pins to "after." Default for OrderedBatch and
  UnorderedBatch — after (safe) or before (throughput)?
  2. None mode + push sources: confirm today's PushBuffer RAM-only behavior is the intent, no special rejection.
  3. Deterministic UUIDv7 for pull — do we expose the derivation as a connector-pluggable hook (so e.g. Kafka source can use (topic, partition, offset) but
  Postgres CDC can use LSN directly) or bake a single hash(source+partition+offset) formula?

=============================
claude's response:

 Three good points. Let me work through them.

  UUIDv7 for pull sources — the determinism vs monotonicity tradeoff

  You identified the exact tension: pure determinism gives you replay identity, microtime+counter gives you sort-order, but they fight each other — adding
  current-microtime breaks replay identity (same event crashes, re-polls, gets a new UUID → sink dedup fails).

  But we don't have to pick. UUIDv7 has room for both if we use the source event's timestamp, not processing microtime:

  UUIDv7 layout (128 bits):
   [ unix_ts_ms : 48 ][ ver:4 ][ rand_a:12 ][ var:2 ][ rand_b:62 ]

  Proposed derivation for pull sources:
  - unix_ts_ms (48) ← source event timestamp (Kafka record ts, Postgres LSN→ts, MySQL binlog ts, Mongo oplog ts). Time-ordered, monotonic by source,
  preserved across replay.
  - rand_a (12) ← (broker_offset & 0xFFF). Monotonic within-partition (offsets always increase), covers 4096 events per ms per partition — comfortably above
   20M/s divided across partitions.
  - rand_b (62) ← SHA-256 of (source_name ‖ partition ‖ offset) truncated to 62 bits. Collision-resistant, deterministic.

  Properties:
  - Time-sortable by source event time (the correct reference frame — processing time is irrelevant to event order).
  - Within-partition monotonic via offset in rand_a, so replay still produces an ordered stream.
  - Fully deterministic — same (source, partition, offset) → same UUID forever, across crashes and reassignments. Sink idempotency-key dedup works.
  - No microtime, no counter, no persisted state. Cost is one SHA-256 per event — already cheap enough at 20M ev/s (hardware-accelerated, ~30 ns).

  Your instinct about microtime+counter is right for push sources (no broker offset to hash), and that's what I already proposed. The split is clean:

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                 Source mode                  │ UUIDv7 timestamp  │                 UUIDv7 random bits                 │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Pull (Kafka, CDC, Redis, file)               │ source event ts   │ offset in rand_a + hash(src,part,offset) in rand_b │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Push (WS, WT, webhook, MQTT, RabbitMQ, QUIC) │ current microtime │ standard PRNG + sub-ms counter for monotonicity    │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Poll (see below)                             │ current microtime │ standard PRNG + sub-ms counter                     │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  HTTP-polling as a third sub-classification

  Agreed — it's not push, and it's pull-shaped but without a durable broker offset to hash. Proposing a third category:

  - pull — Aeon polls, broker has durable position (Kafka offset, CDC LSN, Redis stream ID, file byte offset). No L2 body storage; replay via Seekable.
  - push — upstream initiates (WS, WT, webhook, MQTT, RabbitMQ push, QUIC). L2 body storage required for durability.
  - poll — Aeon polls, but no durable upstream position (HTTP poll against arbitrary REST endpoints, DNS poll, any time-sampled source). L2 body storage
  required, identity minted like push.

  This maps cleanly onto existing trait: Source stays as the trait; add a source_kind() -> SourceKind { Pull, Push, Poll } method so the pipeline runner
  knows whether to engage the L2 write path.

  None mode clarification — yes

  You read it correctly. durability: none means:
  - Push sources: events in PushBuffer RAM only, lost on crash.
  - Poll sources: same.
  - Pull sources: always safe because broker is the buffer of record.
  - ReplayOrchestrator stays RAM-only (the 4(a) graceful path).

  All documented in code comments + docs as "best-effort, not durable." Crash in this mode = accepted loss.

  Push-source ack boundary — yes

  Your rule is now frozen: ack to upstream (webhook 2xx / WS pong / WT stream continue / MQTT PUBACK / AMQP ack) happens after L2 append completes, in any
  mode where durability is enabled. This is what makes the durability claim honest — the upstream only learns we have it when we actually do.

  For durability: none on push sources: ack immediately, accept the RAM-loss risk.

  Updated frozen list

  - Source kinds: Pull / Push / Poll (third category added).
  - UUIDv7 derivation:
    - Pull: source_ts + offset-in-rand_a + hash-in-rand_b → deterministic + time-ordered + within-partition monotonic.
    - Push/Poll: current microtime + PRNG + sub-ms counter → monotonic, standard UUIDv7.
  - Push/Poll ack boundary: after L2 append (when durability on); immediate (when durability off).
  - None mode: RAM-only PushBuffer + ReplayOrchestrator, documented as best-effort.
  - All prior frozen items (sink tiers, L2/L3 split, WAL as L3 fallback, no Kafka backend, budget hierarchy, ETL framing).

  Still open

  1. Derivation formula as a connector-pluggable hook vs a single hardcoded hash(source+partition+offset) — lets Postgres CDC use LSN natively instead of
  synthetic offset.
  2. SHA-256 vs a cheaper hash (xxhash3, wyhash) for the rand_b bits. Not security-critical here — we just need collision resistance within a pipeline.
  xxhash3 is ~3–5× faster than hardware SHA-256. Does collision resistance matter, or is rand_b = 62-bit xxhash3(...) fine?
  3. What happens when source_ts is absent (some brokers don't emit it, or it's lying/zero)? Fallback to current microtime for the 48-bit prefix? This
  breaks pure determinism for those sources — acceptable?

✻ Sautéed for 1m 19s

※ recap: We're designing Aeon's native durability layer (L2 event bodies + L3 checkpoints, per-pipeline configurable) to replace Kafka-biased EOS
  assumptions. Next: your call on three open items — pluggable UUIDv7 derivation per connector, SHA-256 vs xxhash3 for rand_b, and fallback when source_ts
  is absent.

=================================
 
claude's response:

 Three good challenges. Let me address each.

  Poll source config surface

  Agreed — poll brings config weight. Minimum needed per Poll source:

  ┌──────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────┐
  │    Config    │                                Purpose                                │    Example    │
  ├──────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
  │ interval     │ Poll cadence                                                          │ 5s            │
  ├──────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
  │ jitter       │ Avoid synchronized thundering herd                                    │ 10%           │
  ├──────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
  │ timeout      │ Per-poll timeout                                                      │ 3s            │
  ├──────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
  │ dedup_window │ Duplicate suppression when upstream re-serves same data               │ 60s           │
  ├──────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
  │ pagination   │ Cursor/page/offset header name if upstream supports it                │ X-Next-Cursor │
  ├──────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
  │ durability   │ Event-body L2 persistence (inherits pipeline default but overridable) │ orderedBatch  │
  └──────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────┘

  The dedup_window is the key one — poll sources often re-serve the same rows; we need a small L2-backed seen-set keyed by content hash to avoid emitting
  duplicates downstream.

  Multi-source ordering in one pipeline

  This is the important catch. Timestamps from different sources are incomparable — Kafka broker time, Postgres LSN→ts, and webhook arrival time don't share
   a clock or even a clock discipline. There is no honest way to merge-sort them.

  Aeon's honest guarantee, frozen:

  - Within a single source partition: strict order preserved (broker order + offset-in-rand_a UUID). This is always true.
  - Across partitions within a source: no order (same rule Kafka enforces).
  - Across sources in a multi-source pipeline: arrival order at the processor's input ring only. No semantic ordering promise.

  If a user actually needs total order across sources, Aeon is the wrong layer — they should merge upstream (into a Kafka topic, for example) and have Aeon
  consume the merged stream as a single source.

  Documented explicitly in the manifest schema + CONNECTORS.md so it's not a surprise. Sinks that need ordering (e.g. DB writes requiring causal order) get
  it per-partition, not per-pipeline.

  Pluggable identity with centralized hashing (not pluggable hashing)

  Compromise that keeps config minimal but respects source-native identity:

  trait Source {
      fn source_kind() -> SourceKind;
      // Returns canonical identity bytes for this event —
      // Kafka: topic||partition||offset
      // Postgres CDC: LSN
      // Mongo: resume_token
      // File: byte_offset
      // HTTP poll: content_hash (for dedup) + poll_index
      fn event_identity(ev: &RawEvent) -> Bytes;
  }

  Pipeline runner centralizes: rand_b = SHA256(source_name ‖ source.event_identity(ev)).take(62 bits). One hashing path, each connector contributes its
  natural identity. Postgres uses LSN natively; Kafka uses topic+partition+offset; Mongo uses resume_token — no synthetic "offset" forced on anyone.

  Missing source_ts — configuration, not exception

  Your rule: minimal config surface, document clearly, measure cost.

  source:
    - kind: kafka
      event_time: broker     # use broker-provided timestamp (default when available)
    - kind: http_polling
      event_time: aeon_ingest  # no upstream ts — mint at ingest (explicitly non-deterministic)
    - kind: webhook
      event_time: header:X-Event-Time  # pull from named header

  Three values, explicit choice:
  - broker: source provides ts. Deterministic on replay. Default for connectors that expose it.
  - aeon_ingest: we stamp at ingest. Required for push + most poll sources. Loses cross-replay determinism (explicitly flagged in docs).
  - header:<name>: pull from a named metadata header. For webhooks/WS that carry upstream-authored timestamps.

  Cost:
  - broker: zero additional work (we're reading the event anyway).
  - aeon_ingest: one Instant::now() call per event ≈ 20 ns on x86_64 (TSC-backed). Within budget.
  - header:<name>: one SmallVec lookup + parse ≈ 50 ns. Also fine.

  No exception paths, no silent fallback. If broker is configured on a connector that can't provide ts, pipeline fails at start with a clear error — not at
  runtime.

  Updated frozen list

  - SourceKind: Pull / Push / Poll.
  - Multi-source ordering: per-partition-within-source only; no cross-source merge; documented.
  - Identity: Source::event_identity() -> Bytes trait method; hashing centralized with SHA-256.
  - event_time config: broker / aeon_ingest / header:<name>; pipeline fails at start if misconfigured.
  - Poll config minimums: interval, jitter, timeout, dedup_window, pagination, durability.
  - All prior frozen items stand.

  Still open

  1. Content-hash dedup window for poll sources — is this Aeon-owned (hash ring in L2) or do we push it to users via an IdempotencyKey that their HTTP
  response must emit? Aeon-owned is more "just works"; user-owned is more honest.
  2. Cross-partition within a source — any user demand you're aware of for merge-sort by source_ts within one source across partitions? Kafka doesn't do it,
   but we've seen the question in design reviews.

✻ Sautéed for 1m 6s

-> will upstream provide "Content-hash dedup window for poll sources"?
-> not a documented one as of "Cross-partition within a source" currently, unless we choose to deal with single consumer or with consumer group (which is stream-specific option)? how do you think?

================================
claude's response:

 Both questions have "it depends, but here's the honest default" answers.

  Content-hash dedup for poll sources — upstream rarely provides

  Reality check on what HTTP APIs actually expose:

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────┐
  │                   Upstream pattern                    │                        What we get                        │          Reliability           │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Well-designed REST with pagination                    │ ETag, Last-Modified, cursor token, X-Next-Page            │ Good — use as identity         │
  │                                                       │                                                           │ directly                       │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Polling a changelog endpoint                          │ Cursor advances, upstream is the source of truth          │ Excellent                      │
  │ (/changes?since=<cursor>)                             │                                                           │                                │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Polling a "current state" endpoint                    │ Whole payload every time, no identity, frequent           │ None                           │
  │                                                       │ duplicates                                                │                                │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Internal/legacy APIs                                  │ Varies wildly, often no metadata at all                   │ None                           │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────┘

  The majority of real-world poll targets fall in the bottom two rows. So if we require upstream cooperation, poll sources will often ship broken.

  Proposal — hybrid, config-driven, prefer upstream when it exists:

  source:
    - kind: http_polling
      url: https://api.example.com/events
      identity:
        mode: cursor           # cursor | etag | content_hash | compound
        cursor_path: $.next    # JSONPath or header name

  - cursor / etag — zero-cost, upstream is authoritative. Use when available.
  - content_hash — Aeon-owned dedup via xxhash3 of normalized payload into a bounded L2 seen-set with TTL window. Cost: ~30 ns/event + L2 write. Only path
  that works when upstream is uncooperative.
  - compound — both, with cursor as primary and content_hash as safety net (recommended default for unknown APIs).

  Default is compound because it degrades gracefully: if upstream gives us a cursor we trust it; if not, content_hash catches duplicates.

  Cross-partition ordering within a source — Aeon isn't consumer-grouped anyway

  You're right that "consumer group" is the wrong lens for Aeon. Per CLAUDE.md the decision is already frozen: manual partition assign, no consumer groups.
  Each partition has exactly one owner node; partition count is immutable after pipeline creation.

  That gives us a clean framing independent of Kafka's terminology:

  ┌──────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │    Pipeline partition config     │    Ordering property     │                  Throughput                  │
  ├──────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ partitions: 1 (single consumer)  │ Total order preserved    │ Bounded by one core                          │
  ├──────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ partitions: N (default parallel) │ Per-partition order only │ Scales linearly (target: 2.5M ev/s/core × N) │
  └──────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  My recommendation: ship only these two today. Don't build cross-partition merge-by-source-ts until a user actually asks. Reasons:

  - Partition is already the parallelism unit — adding bounded merge-wait reintroduces the serialization we deliberately designed out.
  - A laggard partition would stall the merge window, defeating Aeon's "never the bottleneck" target.
  - Users who need total order have two well-documented paths: set partitions: 1, or re-partition upstream so order-sensitive events share a partition key
  (standard streaming practice).

  If a real demand lands later, it can be added as a per-pipeline opt-in flag with a documented latency-for-order tradeoff. No need to speculatively build
  it now.

  Updated frozen list

  - Poll source identity: config-driven cursor / etag / content_hash / compound, default compound; content_hash path uses L2-backed bounded seen-set with
  TTL.
  - Partition ordering: per-partition order only; partitions: 1 for total-order pipelines; no cross-partition merge, no consumer groups.
  - All prior frozen items stand.

  Still open (the short list now)

  1. Pipeline config manifest shape — once the semantics are locked, what does the YAML actually look like end-to-end for a real multi-source + multi-sink
  pipeline? A concrete example would catch any remaining ambiguity.
  2. Backpressure signaling chain in multi-sink pipelines — if one sink stalls but others are keeping up, do we stall the whole pipeline (preserves ledger
  semantics) or let fast sinks race ahead (splits the L2 GC cursor)? Tends to fall out of the design but worth naming.
  3. Prior L3-ACK-TTL code — I still haven't gone back through to see what of the old implementation survives. Want me to do that pass now, or hold until
  we've nailed the remaining config/backpressure questions?

✻ Sautéed for 43s


※ recap: We're designing Aeon's EOS durability layer — L2 for event bodies, L3 for checkpoints, per-pipeline configurable. Next: decide backpressure
  behavior when one sink in a multi-sink pipeline stalls while others keep up.

-> agree with your perspective w.r.t. "Backpressure signaling chain in multi-sink pipelines"
-> pause discussion w.r.t. " Prior L3-ACK-TTL code" for now

claude's response:

 Here's a concrete manifest that exercises every decision we've frozen. Shape evolves today's single-source/sink structure into arrays, adds durability,
  per-source kind/event_time/identity, and per-sink EOS declaration — without changing unrelated fields.

  pipelines:
    - name: orders-enrichment
      # ─── Durability (pipeline-level, inherits to sources/sinks) ─────────
      durability:
        mode: ordered_batch        # none | unordered_batch | ordered_batch | per_event
        l2_max_bytes: 8Gi          # per-pipeline cap; per-partition soft target derived
        checkpoint:
          backend: state_store     # state_store (primary, L3 redb) | wal (explicit) | none
                                   # state_store auto-falls-back to wal on L3 error
          interval: 1s
          retention: 24h
        flush:
          max_pending: 50000
          adaptive: true

      # ─── Partitioning ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
      partitions: 8                # immutable after creation

      # ─── Sources (one or many) ──────────────────────────────────────────
      sources:
        # Pull source — deterministic UUIDv7 from (source, partition, offset)
        - name: orders-kafka
          type: kafka
          kind: pull                         # pull | push | poll
          topics: [orders]
          partitions: [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7]
          identity:
            mode: native                     # use broker-native offset for rand_b
          event_time: broker                 # broker | aeon_ingest | header:<name>
          config:
            bootstrap.servers: "redpanda:9092"

        # Push source — random UUIDv7, ack-after-L2
        - name: returns-webhook
          type: webhook
          kind: push
          listen: "0.0.0.0:8080"
          path: /returns
          identity:
            mode: random                     # random UUIDv7 (push default)
          event_time: header:X-Event-Time    # fall back to aeon_ingest if header absent? NO — fail at start
          ack_boundary: after_l2             # implicit when durability != none
          backpressure:
            phase1_buffer: 1024              # bounded mpsc
            phase3_threshold: 0.8            # → protocol-level flow control (reject with 503)

        # Poll source — content-hash dedup, L2-backed seen-set
        - name: weather-api
          type: http_polling
          kind: poll
          url: "https://api.weather.example/current"
          interval: 30s
          jitter: 10%
          timeout: 5s
          identity:
            mode: compound                   # cursor | etag | content_hash | compound
            cursor_path: $.next_cursor       # primary when present
            content_hash:
              algorithm: xxhash3_64          # safety-net dedup when cursor absent
              window: 5m                     # L2-backed seen-set TTL
          event_time: aeon_ingest            # explicit — upstream provides none

      # ─── Processor (exactly one) ────────────────────────────────────────
      processor:
        name: orders-enricher
        version: "1.4.2"
        tier: t2_wasm                        # t1_native | t2_wasm | t3_webtransport | t4_websocket
        # tier-specific config (wasm here; t3/t4 would have endpoint + auth)
        runtime:
          fuel: 10_000_000
          memory_pages: 256

      # ─── Sinks (one or many) ────────────────────────────────────────────
      # Sink L2 GC cursor = min(per_sink_ack_seq across all sinks).
      # One slow sink stalls the whole pipeline — by design, correctness first.
      sinks:
        # T2 transactional stream — Kafka exactly-once via producer transactions
        - name: enriched-kafka
          type: kafka
          eos_tier: t2_transactional_stream
          topic: orders-enriched
          config:
            bootstrap.servers: "redpanda:9092"
            transactional.id: "aeon-orders-enrichment-enriched"

        # T4 dedup-keyed — Redis Streams with SETNX dedup
        - name: cache-redis
          type: redis_streams
          eos_tier: t4_dedup_keyed
          stream: orders:enriched
          dedup:
            key_prefix: "aeon:dedup:"
            ttl: 1h                          # must exceed replay window + max checkpoint interval
          config:
            url: "redis://cache:6379"

        # T5 idempotency-key receiver — webhook, correctness depends on receiver
        - name: partner-webhook
          type: webhook_sink
          eos_tier: t5_idempotency_key       # documented as best-effort unless receiver honors header
          url: "https://partner.example/events"
          headers:
            Idempotency-Key: "{event.id}"    # UUIDv7 flows through
          retry:
            max_attempts: 5
            backoff: { initial: 100ms, max: 30s, jitter: 20% }

      # ─── Upgrade / deployment strategy ──────────────────────────────────
      upgrade_strategy: blue_green           # drain_swap | blue_green | canary

  What this manifest makes explicit

  - Three source kinds coexist in one pipeline — Kafka pull, webhook push, HTTP poll — each with the right identity/event_time combo. Multi-source ordering
  is per-partition per-source only; documented in the schema, not hidden.
  - Durability at pipeline level, inherited; sources can override if needed later, but default inheritance keeps the common case terse.
  - Sink EOS tiers are declared, not inferred — the engine matches eos_tier against the connector's actual capability at start; mismatch = fail fast.
  - L2 GC cursor = min(per-sink ack) is a consequence of the sinks-array shape; the slow-sink-stalls-pipeline rule falls out naturally.
  - Kafka checkpoint backend is absent — only state_store / wal / none. The deprecation is visible in the schema.

  Two things I deliberately left out (check these are right)

  1. No state.l2.node_max_bytes in the pipeline manifest — that belongs in the Aeon node config (/etc/aeon/config.yaml or env), not in per-pipeline
  manifests. Operators own node-level caps; pipeline authors own pipeline-level caps.
  2. ack_boundary: after_l2 on the webhook source is shown explicitly for illustration. In practice it's implicit from durability.mode != none — we don't
  expect users to set it. Remove from the default schema?

  Want me to refine any section, or are we ready to call the design frozen and start planning the implementation phases?

✻ Crunched for 1m 43s


=============================
 it looks good. I am once again, thinking about multiple source upstreams & sink downstreams, and their utility for a single pipeline with single
  processor. How do you think? do you find any similar usecases?

==============================
claude's response:

Honest answer: multi-source is strongly justified; multi-sink is justified but comes with tradeoffs worth naming so users don't over-use it.

  Multi-source — real use cases

  ┌────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │      Pattern       │                               Example                               │                   Why single pipeline?                   │
  ├────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ CDC fan-in         │ Postgres CDC + MySQL CDC + Mongo oplog → unified schema normalizer  │ All 3 feed the same normalization logic; stateful joins  │
  │                    │                                                                     │ across them                                              │
  ├────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Event enrichment   │ Orders (Kafka pull) + returns (webhook push) + inventory (HTTP      │ Processor state maintained across all three streams      │
  │ merge              │ poll) → enrichment processor                                        │ (e.g. "order_id → enriched record")                      │
  ├────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Log/metric         │ App logs (push) + syscall metrics (pull) + audit events (CDC) →     │ One parsing/tagging engine, one output shape             │
  │ aggregation        │ single normalizer → observability backend                           │                                                          │
  ├────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Migration /        │ Old system (Kafka) + new system (Kafka) → reconciler → diff sink    │ Processor compares the two streams live                  │
  │ dual-read          │                                                                     │                                                          │
  ├────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Cross-domain       │ Click events + purchase events → fraud/attribution processor        │ Requires joined state                                    │
  │ correlation        │                                                                     │                                                          │
  └────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Competitive analogs shipping this pattern today: Vector.dev (DAG, huge real-world log-pipeline deployments), Benthos/Bento (broker input composition),
  Apache Flink (union), Logstash (multi-input). Kafka Streams and Kafka Connect don't — but both are criticized for that limitation, which is why Flink and
  Vector beat them for complex topologies.

  Multi-sink — real use cases, with tradeoffs

  ┌────────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │            Pattern             │                                       Example                                        │
  ├────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Fan-out to different consumers │ Enriched events → Kafka (downstream streams) + Elasticsearch (search) + S3 (archive) │
  ├────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Tiered storage                 │ Hot (Redis) + warm (Kafka) + cold (S3) from same stream                              │
  ├────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Audit + primary                │ Primary write (Postgres) + immutable audit log (Merkle-chained Kafka topic)          │
  ├────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Multi-region replication       │ Single processor → us-east sink + eu-west sink + ap-south sink                       │
  ├────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Dev/staging mirroring          │ Prod sink + shadow staging sink for testing new processor logic                      │
  └────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  But multi-sink carries two real costs that single-sink doesn't:

  1. Slowest sink paces the whole pipeline (frozen: L2 GC = min ack cursor). A flaky partner webhook stalls the hot Kafka sink behind it.
  2. Upgrade granularity is pipeline-wide. Blue-green on a 3-sink pipeline cuts over all 3 at once. If only the Elasticsearch mapping changed, you'd still
  drain and re-route the Kafka + S3 sinks too.

  The "when NOT to use multi-sink" guidance

  When each sink has independent consumers, independent failure modes, and independent upgrade cadences — run them as separate pipelines sharing a source.
  Example: don't put "partner-webhook-1" and "partner-webhook-2" in the same pipeline; run two pipelines reading the same Kafka topic.

  When sinks have the same event semantics and couple naturally (primary+audit, hot+warm+cold storage, multi-region same-event) — multi-sink in one pipeline
   is the right call, tradeoffs accepted.

  So the design supports both, with docs steering users:

  - Multi-source: use liberally when processor state requires it.
  - Multi-sink: use when sinks are semantically coupled; split into separate pipelines when they're not.

  Frozen addition

  - Multi-source and multi-sink both first-class in pipeline manifest (per current shape).
  - Docs-level guidance: docs/PIPELINE-DESIGN-GUIDE.md (to be written) covers the "when multi-sink vs multiple pipelines" decision with the tradeoff matrix
  above.
  - No schema change needed — the shape we froze already expresses it correctly.

  Does this settle the multi-source/sink question, or is there a specific use case you're still weighing?

※ recap: Designing Aeon's exactly-once durability layer (L2 event bodies + L3 checkpoints, multi-source/sink pipeline manifest) before writing code. Just
  confirmed multi-source and multi-sink are both justified with docs guiding when to split. Next: confirm design is frozen and move to implementation
  phasing.
