  Wrote 2 memories (ctrl+o to expand)

● Reframing with what DO actually offers

  With Regular SSD + 2 Gbps as the ceiling, the math changes:

  ┌──────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │        Bottleneck        │                           Ceiling on this infra                            │
  ├──────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Network per node         │ 2 Gbps = 250 MB/s → ~250K ev/s at 1 KiB/event                              │
  ├──────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 3-node aggregate network │ ~750K ev/s shared across Aeon↔Redpanda + Raft replication + client traffic │
  ├──────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Regular SSD fsync        │ ~5K–20K IOPS → caps Redpanda at 100K–500K msg/s/broker                     │
  ├──────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ PerEvent durability      │ Will stay in 10K–50K ev/s range — same regime as local laptop              │
  └──────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  We will not hit Aeon's ceiling on this infra. The laptop numbers (<2K PerEvent, <40K OrderedBatch) likely reflect the same class of SSD + CPU combo; DOKS
  with dedicated CPU will beat them 2–5×, but not 100×. That's the reality.

  What DOKS can still prove (Gate 2 correctness rows)

  - ✅ Cluster formation — 3-node Raft quorum, leader election, mTLS
  - ✅ Failover < 5 s (T6 chaos interleave)
  - ✅ Cutover < 100 ms (T4) — latency, not throughput
  - ✅ Scale up/down zero-loss (T2/T3) — correctness across transitions
  - ✅ Split-brain drill (T5) — Raft refuses minority commits
  - ✅ PoH chain continuity across transfers
  - ✅ WAL fallback path engages cleanly
  - ✅ Relative 3× multiplier — single-node C2 vs 3-node C2, whatever the absolute number

  What DOKS cannot prove (and we stop pretending it can)

  - ❌ Aeon's absolute throughput ceiling — infra is too constrained
  - ❌ Useful PerEvent numbers — SSD-fsync-bound, same regime as laptop
  - ❌ Anything beyond 250K–500K ev/s per-node aggregate

  Recommendation — split into two sessions

  Session A (today, DOKS): correctness + multiplier, not ceiling

  - Aeon pool: 3 × whichever General Purpose or CPU-Optimized droplet is the largest dedicated-CPU SKU you actually see on the panel. Something like 8
  dedicated vCPU / 16 GB (CPU-Optimized) or 8 dedicated vCPU / 32 GB (General Purpose). Dedicated CPU is the non-negotiable part — shared-CPU s-* ruins
  latency measurements.
  - Redpanda pool: 3 × same class, 4 dedicated vCPU minimum. No point going Storage Optimized if there's no NVMe option.
  - Default pool: 1 × s-2vcpu-4gb, shared CPU, fine for Chaos Mesh / Grafana / loadgen.

  Test plan stays the same (T0–T6), but the plan doc labels absolute numbers as "floor on commodity SSD/2 Gbps" and does not treat them as Aeon's ceiling.

  Session B (post-v0.1, AWS EKS): absolute ceiling claim

  - AWS i4i.2xlarge or i4i.4xlarge — verified available: 8/16 vCPU, local NVMe SSD (~1.8–3.7 TB), up to 18.75 Gbps network. i4i is AWS's current-gen
  storage-optimized line with local NVMe — still listed in AWS docs, not rumored.
  - This is the rig that can actually measure Aeon's real ceiling + validate CPU pinning (EKS supports cpu-manager-policy=static).
  - Becomes one cohesive post-v0.1 session: ceiling numbers + CPU pinning validation + any advanced tuning.

  Question for you before I finalize specs

  Can you open the DO droplet panel and tell me which dedicated-CPU SKUs are actually available to you? Specifically whether you see any of:

  - c-8 (CPU-Optimized, 8 vCPU / 16 GB)
  - g-8vcpu-32gb (General Purpose, 8 vCPU / 32 GB)
  - g-4vcpu-16gb (General Purpose, 4 vCPU / 16 GB)
  - Any m-* Memory Optimized variant

  Once I know the actual SKU names + sizes you see, I'll:
  1. Update docs/GATE2-ACCEPTANCE-PLAN.md with the realistic infra + ceiling-vs-floor framing
  2. Add a short "Session B — AWS EKS ceiling test" section to the same doc (scoped, not planned in detail yet)
  3. Generate the helm values, Redpanda values, namespace manifests, loadgen job, and runbook with the exact SKU you pick

=====================
 it is there in Amsterdam (AMS3) region and not in BLR1. I am really concerned in the context of if digitalocean's previous network limits of 207000
  requests/second reverts due to 2Gbps local node limits!!! thinking....

======================
claude's response:

Your concern is well-grounded. Here's how I'd resolve it without guessing.

  What I actually know vs don't

  Known (roughly): DO historically had a per-droplet packets-per-second cap in the ~200–250K range on the standard (non-Premium) tier, independent of the
  Gbps bandwidth number. The 207K figure you recall matches that range.

  Don't know with certainty: whether the current General Purpose / Storage Optimized droplets in BLR1 still inherit that cap, or whether DO has raised it
  silently on the dedicated-CPU classes. Their public docs are inconsistent on this point.

  What it means if the cap is real at ~207K PPS

  At 1 KiB events ≈ 1 packet per event:
  - Per-node event ceiling: ~200K ev/s (PPS-bound, not bandwidth-bound)
  - 3-node aggregate: ~400–500K ev/s, not the ~750K I sketched earlier
  - OrderedBatch likely lands 150–300K ev/s per node — still 4–8× laptop, so the relative-scaling signal survives, but the floor drops further

  Three options

  Option 1 — Pre-flight PPS probe (cheap, 30 min, ~$1)
  Provision 2 × General Purpose 8 vCPU droplets in BLR1 first, run iperf3 -u -b 2G -l 64 both directions, see actual sustained PPS. If ≥ 500K PPS, cap
  doesn't apply — proceed as planned. If ~200K PPS, cap is real — decide between Options 2 and 3. Destroy the two droplets after the probe.

  Option 2 — Switch to AMS3 for the Premium tier
  Gets NVMe + 10 Gbps + high PPS (the real rig). Cost roughly doubles (~$40 same-day vs ~$20). kubectl latency from India ~150 ms — annoying but not
  blocking; test traffic is all inside AMS3. This path also lets us run CPU pinning validation today instead of deferring to EKS. You'd get an actual
  ceiling number out of today.

  Option 3 — Accept the cap, stay in BLR1
  DOKS session is explicitly correctness-only; absolute numbers noted as "DO standard-tier network floor." Ceiling claim defers entirely to AWS EKS
  post-v0.1.

  My recommendation

  Option 1 first — $1 to definitively answer the PPS question, 30 min of your time. Then:
  - If probe clears it (≥ 500K PPS sustained): proceed with BLR1 Session A as already planned.
  - If probe confirms the cap: Option 2 (AMS3 Premium) is my preference. You already accepted the 2× cost bump mentally for Gate 2; doubling $20 → $40 for a
   real ceiling number is the better deal than deferring entirely. And you save the CPU-pinning EKS trip.

  What do you want — run the probe, or pick a region/tier now based on the info at hand?

Session A — scope (DOKS AMS3, same-day)

  From the plan doc, Session A tests are:

  - PPS probe (prerequisite, § 3.6) — ~30 min, ~$1
  - T0 isolation matrix, 12 cells (4 durability modes × 3 topologies) — ~60 min
  - T1 3-node throughput at 3× single-node, 4 modes — ~20 min
  - T2 scale-up 1→3→5, zero loss — ~15 min
  - T3 scale-down 5→3→1, zero loss — ~15 min
  - T4 cutover measurement (< 100 ms target) — ~5 min
  - T5 split-brain drill (Chaos Mesh) — ~10 min
  - T6 10-min sustained run with chaos interleave — ~15 min

  Active test time ~2.5 hr. Add ~1 hr provisioning + ~30 min teardown + ~1 hr buffer for code gaps → ~5 hours wall-clock.

  Session B — scope + hours (AWS EKS, weekend)

  Only what DOKS cannot do:

  1. T1 at ceiling — Redpanda→Redpanda, 4 durability modes, on NVMe + 18.75–25 Gbps. Rate-sweep until Aeon CPU genuinely hits 50 %, not until infra
  saturates first. ~60 min.
  2. CPU pinning validation — enable cpu-manager-policy=static on EKS kubelet, re-run T1 with pinned Guaranteed-QoS pods, measure delta. ~90 min (kubelet
  reconfig + restart + re-run).
  3. T6 sustained at ceiling — 30-min run at 80 % of T1 ceiling. ~45 min.

  Active test time ~3.5 hr. Add ~60 min provisioning + ~15 min teardown + ~60 min buffer → ~6 hours wall-clock, which matches the $40 / 6-hr budget I listed
   earlier. Hard cap $50 at ~7.5 hr.

   doesn't have NVMe or the pinning knob.

  ================
  claude's response:
  
  Re: the $73 and $150 figures — how AWS actually bills you

  AWS line items are hourly, prorated. The monthly numbers you see in AWS Calculator are just hourly × 730 hours. If you tear down after 6 hours, you pay
  6/730 of that.

  EKS control plane ($73/mo):
  - Actual rate: $0.10/hr per cluster
  - 6-hour Session B: $0.60
  - 24-hour weekend: $2.40
  - Full month if left running: $73
  - Already included in my earlier $6.43/hr figure.

  $150 TLS cost — I don't recognize that as a standard AWS line item. Possibilities, so you can tell me which one you saw:

  ┌───────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────┬─────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │       What it might be        │         Rate          │  6-hr cost  │                                     Notes                                     │
  ├───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────┼─────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Public ACM certs (ALB/NLB     │ $0                    │ $0          │ Free forever. This is what 99 % of workloads use.                             │
  │ TLS)                          │                       │             │                                                                               │
  ├───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────┼─────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ ACM Private CA (root CA)      │ $400/mo               │ $3.28       │ Only needed if issuing internal certs via AWS. Aeon mTLS uses rustls +        │
  │                               │                       │             │ internal CA — not needed.                                                     │
  ├───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────┼─────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ ACM Private CA short-lived    │ $50/mo                │ $0.41       │ Same caveat — not needed for our test.                                        │
  │ mode                          │                       │             │                                                                               │
  ├───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────┼─────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Network Load Balancer + data  │ ~$0.03/hr/LB +        │ ~$0.20      │ Only if we front-end with AWS LB. Internal Aeon/Redpanda traffic doesn't need │
  │ proc                          │ $0.008/GB             │             │  it.                                                                          │
  ├───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────┼─────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ CloudHSM                      │ $1.45/hr              │ $8.70       │ FIPS-required workloads. Not needed for Gate 2.                               │
  ├───────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────┼─────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

  └───────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────┴─────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  │   └── ...
  ├── vke/                         # optional — if you want to price-check Vultr later
  │   └── ...
  └── hetzner/                     # future — Talos machineconfig for self-managed
      └── ...

  Principles:
  - One helm values file per provider (never share values between DOKS and EKS — they disagree on storage class names, tolerations, pinning annotations)
  - shared/ is the exact same YAML applied on every cluster (Chaos Mesh, Prometheus, topic-create, loadgen)
  - test-scripts/ reads $KUBECONFIG and works identically on any provider — that's where the rebuild-speed gain comes from
  - Move the current helm/aeon/values-doks.yaml into deploy/doks/values-aeon.yaml and deprecate the old path

✻ Crunched for 55s


==================
 I meant about wildcard certificate generation with AWS Certificate Manager.

==================
claude's response:

 AWS ACM wildcard certificates — actually free

  AWS Certificate Manager public wildcard certs are $0. Amazon issues them at no charge:

  - *.yourdomain.com wildcard: free
  - Auto-renews: free
  - Requires domain ownership (DNS validation via CNAME record or email)

  The only ACM line item that costs real money is Private CA ($400/mo), and that's a completely different product for issuing internal certificates — not
  relevant here.

  Caveat that probably matters more for Gate 2

  ACM certs only bind to AWS services — ALB, NLB, CloudFront, API Gateway. They cannot be mounted directly into a Kubernetes pod for Aeon/Redpanda
  pod-to-pod mTLS. The certificate private key never leaves AWS; you can only terminate TLS at an AWS load balancer using it.

  For the Gate 2 test (Aeon inter-node Raft over QUIC + Redpanda broker mTLS), you need certs inside the pods. Your options on EKS:

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    Approach                     │          Cost           │                             Fit for Gate 2                              │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ cert-manager + self-signed internal CA (inside  │ $0                      │ Best fit — matches Phase 10 rustls + internal CA design. Same mechanism │
  │ cluster)                                        │                         │  as DOKS.                                                               │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ cert-manager + Let's Encrypt wildcard via Route │ $0.50/mo Route 53       │ Only useful if nodes need public-valid certs. Overkill for              │
  │  53                                             │ hosted zone             │ intra-cluster.                                                          │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ ACM wildcard on NLB (TLS terminates at LB,      │ $0 cert + ~$16/mo NLB   │ Breaks the Gate 2 story — we're specifically testing end-to-end mTLS    │
  │ plaintext to pods)                              │                         │ between pods. Don't use.                                                │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Bottom line: wildcard ACM is $0, and for Session B you don't need it at all — cert-manager inside the cluster with a self-signed internal CA covers Aeon
  mTLS for free and matches what the DOKS cluster already does. No $150 line, no $73 line past the hourly $0.10 EKS fee, Session B stays at ~$39 for 6 hr /
  ~$154 for 24 hr.

  Want me to proceed with the deploy/ scaffold now that cost picture is clear?

  confirmed free. Next: create the deploy/ scaffold with per-provider config subdirs.

  3. Use larger event payloads (e.g. 4 KiB) to reduce PPS for the same throughput and sidestep the cap concern.

 No .ssh directory on this Windows machine — the loadtest-key private key isn't available locally, so the SSH-based probe script won't work as written.

  Practical pivot I'd recommend (costs less, uses infra you're already paying for):

  Skip the standalone probe. Instead, bake the PPS check into T0 by running C2 (Redpanda→Redpanda) at both 1 KiB and 4 KiB event sizes:

  - If 1 KiB throughput is ~207K ev/s and 4 KiB is ~4× higher in ev/s-equivalent bytes, we've hit the PPS cap — document and shift to 4 KiB for T1.
  - If 1 KiB throughput goes well above 207K ev/s, cap isn't active — proceed at 1 KiB as planned.
  - Either way, we get the answer via tests we were going to run anyway, using the cluster already burning $2.53/hr.

  Alternative if you want the strict probe: generate a fresh SSH key on this machine, upload to DO, use it for 2 throwaway droplets. ~10 min extra setup
  before the probe itself.

  To actually run T0 we need two things the cluster doesn't have yet:
  1. A pipeline manifest for each topology (C0 Memory→Blackhole, C1 Redpanda→Blackhole, C2 Redpanda→Redpanda), swept across 4 durability modes → configured
  per-cell via the REST API

  Two ways to proceed:

  - (a) Manual per-cell run — I write one pipeline YAML per durability mode, kubectl exec into aeon-0 to POST the pipeline config to /pipelines (or wherever
   the REST route is — I need to look up the actual route), run rpk produce, record throughput from /metrics or a Prometheus scrape. Fine but slow per cell.
  - (b) Scripted T0 driver — write deploy/shared/test-scripts/t0-isolation.sh that iterates all 12 cells, starts/stops each pipeline, runs the load,
  captures results to a JSON file. More work upfront, dramatically faster to re-run, future-proof for Session B.

  Given Session A is the first of (at least) two sessions and T0 shape is what we'll also need on EKS, I'd recommend (b). Writing the driver is ~30 min of
  work and saves hours over the course of both sessions.

  Want me to:
  1. First find the actual Aeon REST API routes (grep crates/aeon-engine/src/rest_api.rs) so the driver script can POST valid pipelines?
  2. Then write t0-isolation.sh with all 12 cells?
  3. Then run cell C0-None as a single-cell smoke test before committing to the full 60-min run?

  Or pause here and you want to review cluster state / bail out / redirect?

  Gaps blocking T0

  ┌─────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  #  │                                                   Gap                                                   │              Evidence               │
  ├─────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┤
  │     │ No pipeline runner on Running transition. PipelineManager.start() just flips a state flag               │ pipeline_manager.rs:83-104 — state  │
  │ 1   │ (pipeline_manager.rs:77-105). Nothing watches state changes or spawns run_buffered_managed. The data    │ flip only; no task spawn            │
  │     │ plane exists but is only wired into aeon dev watch (hardcoded TickSource+StdoutSink) and tests/benches. │                                     │
  ├─────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┤
  │     │ No connector factory. REST carries source_type: "kafka"/"memory" strings but nothing maps those to      │ Grep: no ConnectorRegistry /        │
  │ 2   │ concrete impl Source/impl Sink. Connectors exist (memory/, blackhole/, kafka/ etc.) but there's no      │ build_source(config) anywhere       │
  │     │ registry.                                                                                               │                                     │
  ├─────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┤
  │     │ Durability dropped in manifest → definition. PipelineManifest.durability.mode (the enum we need) is     │ manifest.rs:425-437 — no durability │
  │ 3   │ parsed, but to_pipeline_definition() (manifest.rs:377-438) doesn't carry it into PipelineDefinition. T0 │  field written                      │
  │     │  literally cannot sweep durability modes over REST today.                                               │                                     │
  ├─────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ 4   │ Image has no curl — minor; port-forward from driver host works.                                         │ debian-slim default                 │
  └─────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────┘

  Three options for closing 1–3

  A. Build the full bridge (product-grade). ConnectorRegistry trait, PipelineSupervisor task watching PipelineManager state, add durability to
  PipelineDefinition, map to DeliveryConfig. ~1-2 days. Unblocks every future Gate and is inevitable anyway.

  B. Bootstrap YAML on pod start (T0-scoped shortcut). aeon serve reads AEON_BOOTSTRAP_PIPELINES=/config/t0.yaml, hard-dispatches the 3 source/sink pairs T0
   needs, spawns run_buffered_managed directly. ~4 hours. Disposable; thrown away when A lands.

  C. Use existing eo2_durability_bench on-pod. Already wires MemorySource→BlackholeSink × all 4 modes. Unblocks 4/12 T0 cells today, but C1
  (Redpanda→Blackhole) and C2 (Redpanda→Redpanda) still need A or B.

  My recommendation: A. Reasons: (a) it's inevitable for every Gate ≥2, (b) Option B's throwaway code burns ~half of A's effort and leaves no residual
  value, (c) our "stay on task" memory cuts against B anyway — the task is closing T0, and you can't close T0 without this wiring. Option C can run in
  parallel on C0 while A lands for C1/C2.

  Which do you want? If A, I'll draft the design before writing code.

===============
claude's response:

Drafting the design first.

  Design — Manifest → Running Pipeline Bridge

  Crate-level changes

  aeon-types/registry.rs — add to PipelineDefinition:
  pub durability: DurabilityBlock,   // already in aeon-types/manifest.rs
  Default DurabilityBlock::default() so existing serialized PipelineDefinitions deserialize without a migration. Update
  PipelineManifest::to_pipeline_definition() to preserve self.durability.

  aeon-types/traits.rs — no change. Source/Sink stay generic in the hot path; we add Box<dyn> entry points at the supervisor boundary.

  aeon-engine/src/connector_registry.rs (new):
  pub trait SourceFactory: Send + Sync {
      fn build(&self, cfg: &SourceConfig) -> Result<BoxedSource, AeonError>;
  }
  pub trait SinkFactory: Send + Sync {
      fn build(&self, cfg: &SinkConfig) -> Result<BoxedSink, AeonError>;
  }
  pub type BoxedSource = Box<dyn Source + Send + Sync>;
  pub type BoxedSink   = Box<dyn Sink   + Send + Sync>;

  pub struct ConnectorRegistry {
      sources: HashMap<String, Arc<dyn SourceFactory>>,
      sinks:   HashMap<String, Arc<dyn SinkFactory>>,
  }
  Ship with memory, blackhole, kafka registered by default. Others land as the Gate gets to them.

  aeon-engine/src/pipeline_supervisor.rs (new):
  pub struct PipelineSupervisor { /* running: DashMap<String, RunningPipeline> */ }
  impl PipelineSupervisor {
      pub async fn start(&self, def: PipelineDefinition) -> Result<()> { /* build, spawn run_buffered_managed_dyn */ }
      pub async fn stop(&self,  name: &str) -> Result<()> { /* shutdown flag + join */ }
      pub async fn reconcile(&self, defs: &BTreeMap<String, PipelineDefinition>) { /* for Raft-driven converge */ }
  }

  aeon-engine/src/pipeline.rs — add run_buffered_managed_dyn(BoxedSource, Box<dyn Processor>, BoxedSink, ...) that delegates to the existing generic for a
  concrete struct DynSource(BoxedSource) / DynSink(BoxedSink) pair, so we don't duplicate the whole function body. Trait-object safety confirmed via a
  compile test (Source and Sink use async fn — will need #[async_trait] wrapper or BoxFuture at the dyn boundary).

  REST wiring (rest_api.rs) — start_pipeline and stop_pipeline handlers gain supervisor calls after the state-flip. create_pipeline validates the
  source/sink types against the registry.

  cmd_serve (aeon-cli) — constructs ConnectorRegistry::default(), PipelineSupervisor::new(), stashes both in AppState.

  Durability plumbing

  PipelineDefinition.durability → mapped inside spawn_pipeline() into PipelineConfig.delivery:
  - durability.mode → delivery.durability
  - durability.checkpoint.backend → delivery.checkpoint.backend (StateStore/Wal/None)
  - durability.checkpoint.interval → delivery.flush.interval (parse "1s" → Duration)
  - durability.flush.max_pending / adaptive → delivery.flush.*

  For T0, we only exercise mode. The other fields stay at defaults until T2/T3.

  Passthrough processor for T0

  T0's pipelines have no transformation. Add one minimal built-in: IdentityProcessor that returns Output{destination: default_sink, payload:
  event.payload.clone()}. Registered with ProcessorRegistry under reserved name __identity so manifests can reference it without registering an artifact.

  Open questions I'm resolving as I go

  1. Raft replication of state changes — Supervisor reconciles on the local node from the Raft-applied pipeline state; I'll hook it off
  ClusterRegistryApplier (aeon-cli/src/main.rs:547) so every follower spawns/stops on commit, not just the leader. This is the right semantics: each pod
  owns its own partitions' data plane.
  2. Partition assignment — for T0 all 3 pods run the same pipeline name, but each pod should only consume the partitions assigned to it by the leader.
  Supervisor passes assigned_node/partition-table info to the Kafka source builder. Good enough for C1/C2; Memory source ignores partitions.
  3. DurabilityBlock string parsing — interval: "1s" needs humantime. Crate already in deps? If not, I'll add it or do a tiny parser.

  Ordered implementation steps

  1. Add DurabilityBlock field to PipelineDefinition; update to_pipeline_definition().
  2. Create ConnectorRegistry + factories for memory, blackhole, kafka.
  3. Create run_buffered_managed_dyn adapter.
  4. Create PipelineSupervisor (start/stop/reconcile).
  5. Add IdentityProcessor + register under reserved name.
  6. Wire into cmd_serve + AppState.
  7. Hook start/stop REST handlers.
  8. Hook ClusterRegistryApplier for Raft-driven reconcile on followers.
  9. Unit tests + one integration test that creates a Memory→Blackhole pipeline via REST and verifies events flow.
  10. Rebuild image, redeploy, smoke-test.

  Want me to start with step 1 now, or tighten anything in the design first?

   Aeon ignores it → SIGKILL after 30s → events lost. Let me check the binary + Dockerfile entrypoint to confirm this is the path.

  axum graceful shutdown

The full shutdown sequence fires as designed:
  1. received SIGTERM → signal handler caught K8s SIGTERM

  3. waiting for endpoint propagation before stopping pipelines secs=5 → sleep

 158K PPS is below both the 500K target and the historical 207K cap — but this is pod-to-pod through the CNI overlay, not raw node-to-node. Let me run the
  reverse direction and also a hostNetwork test for comparison:


 Both directions ~157-158K PPS. Now testing hostNetwork (bypass CNI) to isolate whether this is a DO droplet cap or CNI overhead:

 PPS probe results — AMS3 aeon-pool (g-8vcpu-32gb)

  ┌───────────────────────────┬─────────┬─────────────┬────────┐
  │         Scenario          │   PPS   │ Throughput  │  Loss  │
  ├───────────────────────────┼─────────┼─────────────┼────────┤
  │ Pod→pod CNI, 64B UDP, A→B │ 158,202 │ 0.08 Gbit/s │  0.04% │
  ├───────────────────────────┼─────────┼─────────────┼────────┤
  │ Pod→pod CNI, 64B UDP, B→A │ 156,915 │ 0.08 Gbit/s │  0.03% │
  ├───────────────────────────┼─────────┼─────────────┼────────┤
  │ hostNetwork raw, 64B UDP  │ 174,908 │ 0.09 Gbit/s │  0.10% │
  ├───────────────────────────┼─────────┼─────────────┼────────┤
  │ hostNetwork, 1400B UDP    │ 199,795 │ 2.24 Gbit/s │ 13.24% │
  └───────────────────────────┴─────────┴─────────────┴────────┘

  Against pps-probe.sh criteria: ≥500K = FAIL · 200–400K = FAIL · ≤207K (historical cap active) = THIS.

  Interpretation

  - DO PPS cap is real and binding at ~175K PPS raw, ~157K PPS through the CNI overlay (~10% overhead). Matches the historical 207K ceiling.
  - Bandwidth is not the bottleneck — 2.24 Gbit/s at MTU-size proves the droplets have plenty of byte throughput; only the per-packet rate is capped.
  - Aeon's hot path is batched Kafka — rdkafka coalesces 100–1000 events per TCP segment. At 256 B/event, 157K packets/s → millions of events/s. PPS cap
  won't bind for our workload.
  - It will bind for: WebTransport single-event fanout, QUIC partition-handover bursts of tiny control frames, and any test that measures raw packet rate
  rather than event rate.

  Recommendation: proceed with Session A

  Session A's purpose is cluster correctness (election, drain, split-brain, checkpoint recovery) — none of it is PPS-bound. The throughput rows that could
  be PPS-sensitive should be read as "event throughput through batched Kafka," which stays well below the cap. I'll note the ~175K droplet-level PPS ceiling
   next to any measurement that touches tiny-packet paths (T3 WebTransport, T5 cluster QUIC fanout) so results are interpreted correctly.

  Next steps if you agree:
  1. Tear down pps-probe namespace
  2. Log these numbers inline in docs/GATE2-ACCEPTANCE-PLAN.md § 10
  3. Install Redpanda (redpanda ns) → Aeon (aeon ns) → Prometheus (monitoring pool)
  4. Run the Session A matrix

  Shall I proceed?

  Phase 1 — Pre-respin code bundle (must land before next DOKS)

  #: 1c

  Where: new aeon-cluster/src/transfer_driver.rs; spawned from node.rs
  Sizing: L
  Notes: The headline item. Driver shuts down on leader-loss; no double-orchestration. Reuses existing TransferTracker, no new state machine.

  Phase 3 — Session A re-run on fresh DOKS (single same-day session)

  Use exact same provisioning recipe (deploy/doks/values-aeon.yaml, helm/aeon chart, Redpanda 26.1.2). Run order — fail-fast:

  1. Smoke: cluster status, Raft membership, Prometheus 9/9.

  3. T1 regression: 3-node throughput.

  don't burn cluster on T2/T3.

  6. T3: scale 5 → 3 → 1 via aeon cluster drain.

  follower-read-only, both reconverge after partition heals.

  9. T0.C2.PerEvent (was deferred): now possible with G3.
  10. Tear down.

  Cost: ~$15–20 same-day per Session 0 history.

claude's response:

Here's my honest walk-through. I'm calling out things I'm uncertain about rather than rubber-stamping — easier to negotiate now than mid-implementation.

  Phase 1 — open design questions worth resolving before I touch code

  #48 (1a — write-freeze API): gate primitive choice.
  I wrote "Arc" in the task description, but that's too crude. Setting a flag doesn't tell the cutover caller when the in-flight batch has actually drained.
   We need either tokio::sync::Notify + counter, or tokio::sync::watch<GateState> so drain_and_freeze can await real completion. The gate also needs to
  apply per (pipeline, partition) and not interfere with sibling partitions on the same pipeline. → Decide: do we want async-drain semantics with a real
  completion signal, or fire-and-forget freeze with a fixed-timeout sleep? Async-drain is correct but +1 day of work.

  #49 (1b — CutoverCoordinator impl): trait might be sync today.
  The investigation reported the trait as fn drain_and_freeze(...) -> Result<CutoverOffsets, AeonError> — synchronous. If that's accurate, we can't await
  drain inside it. Either we change the trait to async fn (touches CL-6 transport call sites) or we block-on-runtime inside the impl (ugly). → Pre-flight
  check: I should read transport/cutover.rs:43-48 myself before starting, and if it's sync, propose changing it to async as part of 1b.

  #49 also has a sub-question: where do final_source_offset and final_poh_sequence come from?
  PoH is engine-side, that's fine. But "source offset" is connector-specific — a Kafka offset for Kafka source, a counter for Memory source, doesn't even
  exist for HTTP push source. → Decide: is final_source_offset a u64 opaque to the cluster layer (connector-defined), or do we narrow to "Kafka-only
  handover at first"? I'd vote opaque u64 with 0 for sources that don't have one.

  #51 (1d — G1): scope is bigger than I sized.
  Injecting a cluster handle into KafkaSourceFactory requires plumbing through SourceFactory::build() signature, which currently doesn't take a cluster
  context. That's a small refactor across aeon-cli + the factory traits. Also: when a partition transfers AT RUNTIME (post-G2), should the running source
  dynamically re-subscribe? rdkafka manual-assign is one-shot — we'd need a re-assign hook. → Decide: G1 only handles startup defaulting (reads ownership at
   source-build time), and dynamic re-assign on transfer is a separate item we punt to "after Phase 3 reveals it as needed"? I'd vote yes — keep G1 scoped.

  #53 (1f — G4): per-sink ack callback or generic?
  Each sink type emits ack signals differently (Kafka delivery report, HTTP response, Blackhole immediate). Cleanest is a generic Sink::on_ack(callback)
  trait method, but that's a trait change rippling across 8+ implementations. Quick-and-dirty is to bump the metric inline in each sink's delivered path. →
  Decide: do this metric properly via a trait method, or fast-and-targeted (Kafka sink only for now, since that's the only one where the gap is visible)?
  I'd vote Kafka-only for Phase 1 and document the limitation.

  Phase 2 — one ambiguity

  #59 (2d — T2 scale "1→3→5"): cluster doesn't start at 1.
  The original test name in GATE2-ACCEPTANCE-PLAN.md is "1→3→5", but our DOKS cluster is born at 3 (StatefulSet replicas: 3). To honestly test 1→3, we'd
  need to deploy with replicas:1, then scale to 3 (Raft membership change), then 5. That's a much bigger test and the membership-change path is its own can
  of worms. → Decide: rename to "3→5 expansion" + "5→3→1 contraction" (T3) and skip the 1→3 step, or do the full 1→3→5 with chart re-deploy in between? I'd
  vote scoped — 3→5 only, document the deferral.

  #60 (2e — T6): chaos target specifics missing.
  Should be explicit: PodChaos kills aeon-1 (random follower) at t=3, NetworkChaos partitions aeon-0 from {aeon-1, aeon-2} at t=6. Update the task
  description to say so.

  Phase 3 — one addition

  #61 missing pre-flight gate: add as the first step "Run cargo test -p aeon-cluster transfer_driver_e2e locally; if it passes, then provision DOKS." Saves
  $20 if Phase 1 has a regression.

  Phase 4 — two real concerns

  #62 (4.i — region ambiguity). I read your "same region" as confirming my recommendation of us-east-1. But it could equally mean "same as DOKS" — DOKS was
  AMS3, so AWS-equivalent is eu-west-1 (Ireland) or eu-central-1 (Frankfurt). Cost and SKU availability differ — i4i.4xlarge and c7gn.4xlarge are both
  available in us-east-1, eu-west-1, and eu-central-1, but us-east-1 is cheapest spot. → Confirm: us-east-1 or eu-west-1? Doesn't matter much technically;
  matters for your kubectl latency.

  #64 (4.iii — ARM64 build pipeline missing from scope.) I picked Graviton (c7gn.4xlarge) for the aeon-pool because of cost + 100 Gbps networking. But
  that's arm64 — our Dockerfile builds x86_64. We'd need either (a) docker buildx with QEMU emulation (slow but works), (b) a CI matrix building both
  arches, or (c) switch to x86_64 Aeon nodes (c7n.4xlarge or similar) and lose the Graviton cost edge. → Decide: arm64 build pipeline (extra ~½ day, gets us
   cheap Graviton), or stay x86_64 for Phase 4 and revisit later? I'd vote x86_64 for now — keep Session B mechanical, don't introduce a new build axis at
  the same time.

  Items I considered but did NOT add — flagging for your input

  - Tag a v0.1-rc after Phase 3 passes. Worth a task or just noted in commit?
  - Handle dynamic source re-assign on partition transfer. Almost certainly needed once G2 works; deferred above with G1.
  - Per-sink dynamic checkpointing on transfer. When a partition moves, the checkpoint store on the source node has the latest watermark; the target needs
  to start from there. May already work via existing CL-6a SegmentManifest design — needs verification but I'd defer to Phase 3 evidence.
  - aeon cluster status --watch — operator UX, would have helped during Session A debugging. Low priority, defer.

  Bottom line

  Open decisions I need from you to lock the plan:

  1. #48/49 async-drain semantics — proper await or timeout-and-pray?
  2. #49 final_source_offset shape — opaque u64 or Kafka-only first?
  3. #51 G1 scope — startup-only defaulting (yes) or also dynamic re-assign (defer)?
  4. #53 G4 — per-sink trait method or Kafka-only fast path?
  5. #59 T2 scope — drop "1→3" and just do "3→5"?
  6. #62 region — confirm us-east-1 vs eu-west-1?
  7. #64 arch — arm64 Graviton build pipeline now, or x86_64 for Session B?

  Tell me on each and I'll lock the task descriptions before starting.

-> #48 (1a) yes, let us implement async-drain semantics with a real completion signal

-> #49 also has a sub-question: where do final_source_offset and final_poh_sequence come from? The response for this is, we have discussed to consider a possibility of using source offset as a possibility of deterministic event uniqueness for source connectors with source method as PULL. UUIDv7 to be generated for source connectors with source method as PUSH or POLL. Do check old notes. I am hesitant about using deterministic approach for PULL source method but we discussed and that option was chosen, to avoid writing on Aeon internal storage (L3 or kind of) and instead to use source offset to pull required data for processing, as and when needed, and the only way it can be controlled is since the thought process is to maintain current source offset for each pipeline (at pipeline/partition level kind of).

claude's response:

Let me confirm the source-offset / UUIDv7 design from your old notes before answering, since #49 hinges on it.

 Confirmed — your old design is intact in code. CutoverOffsets already exists (crates/aeon-cluster/src/transport/cutover.rs:29-34) as {
  final_source_offset: i64, final_poh_sequence: u64 } and PULL/PUSH/POLL split is exactly per your description (EO-2 design §5.1, lines 197-205). Now I can
  answer all of your points cleanly.

  Decisions locked in

  #48/49 async-drain semantics ✅ — Notify-based completion signal in 1a; trait flips to async fn drain_and_freeze in 1b (touches CL-6 transport call sites
  — minor).

  #49 source offset shape ✅ — exactly what you described. The plumbing becomes:

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │              Source kind              │        final_source_offset         │                    What target node uses to resume                    │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ PULL (Kafka, file tail, JDBC scan, S3 │ per-partition i64 (the connector's │ Seekable::seek(offset) then resume — no Aeon-side L3 storage needed   │
  │  list-and-fetch...)                   │  native cursor)                    │ for event uniqueness                                                  │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ PUSH (HTTP, gRPC, NATS, MQTT,         │ -1 sentinel (already used at       │ Reads transferred L2 segments from min(per_sink_ack_seq) + 1 forward  │
  │ WebTransport...)                      │ server.rs:238)                     │ (CL-6a SegmentManifest already moves these)                           │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ POLL (DB CDC, periodic API scrape,    │ -1 sentinel                        │ Same L2-replay path as PUSH; content-hash dedup window covers         │
  │ file dir watch...)                    │                                    │ in-flight retransmits                                                 │
  └───────────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  Important point I want to lock in your task description: Kafka is the test fixture, not the design center. I'll write final_source_offset as opaque to the
   cluster layer — connectors define the meaning; cluster only forwards. That keeps the same code path open for any future PULL connector (S3, BigQuery
  export poll, etc.).

  #53 G4 ✅ — generic trait method, validate on Kafka first, expand once green. Slot the trait change as Sink::on_ack(callback: ...) defaulted to no-op, so
  the other 7 sinks compile unchanged and we add real impls connector-by-connector after Phase 3 evidence. Backward-safe.

  #61 pre-flight gate ✅ — added.

  #62 region ✅ — us-east-1 for AWS, AMS3 for DOKS (no change).

  #64 arch ✅ — x86_64.

  No version bump now ✅ — noted.

  Two new tasks I'll add:
  - Dynamic source re-assign on partition transfer (after G2, post-Phase-3 lands as evidence-driven)
  - aeon cluster status --watch (operator UX)

  Open items still to discuss

  #51 — G1 (Kafka source partition defaulting): the four real options

  Current behavior (crates/aeon-cli/src/connectors.rs:116-120):
  let partitions: Vec<i32> = if cfg.partitions.is_empty() {
      vec![0]                  // ← bug
  } else {
      cfg.partitions.iter().map(|p| *p as i32).collect()
  };

  What "empty list" should mean depends on the deployment shape. Four options:

  ┌─────┬──────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  #  │        Option        │       Behavior on partitions: []        │             Pros              │                    Cons                     │
  ├─────┼──────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │     │ Cluster-aware        │ Read locally-owned partitions from      │ Honest cluster behavior;      │ Requires injecting cluster handle into      │
  │ A   │ default (my          │ partition_table_snapshot() on this      │ pipeline JSON is portable     │ source-factory builder (small refactor); if │
  │     │ recommendation)      │ node. Single-node → all partitions.     │ across nodes; no manual list  │  partition table not ready at build time,   │
  │     │                      │                                         │ maintenance                   │ source startup must block until it is       │
  ├─────┼──────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │     │                      │ Empty list returns Err with a clear     │ Zero magic; no race           │ Forces every cluster pipeline JSON to       │
  │ B   │ Fail-fast            │ "specify partitions or use              │ conditions; explicit          │ either enumerate or use a magic "all" token │
  │     │                      │ cluster-aware mode" message.            │                               │  — DX downgrade                             │
  ├─────┼──────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │     │ Read all topic       │ Empty → query Kafka topic metadata,     │ Cluster-agnostic; doesn't     │ Two parallel partition-ownership models     │
  │ C   │ partitions, pod-side │ hash partition_id by pod ordinal,       │ depend on Aeon partition      │ (Kafka's view + Aeon's view) — confusing;   │
  │     │  modulo shard        │ subscribe to its share.                 │ table                         │ doesn't honor the Aeon partition table when │
  │     │                      │                                         │                               │  ownership transfers                        │
  ├─────┼──────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │     │ Consumer groups      │ Drop manual assign, use Kafka consumer  │                               │ Locked out by CLAUDE.md — manual assign is  │
  │ D   │ instead of manual    │ group rebalancing.                      │ Battle-tested by Kafka itself │ a frozen decision because Aeon owns         │
  │     │ assign               │                                         │                               │ partition→node mapping, not Kafka           │
  └─────┴──────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  My pick: A — it's the only option that lets Aeon's own partition ownership be the source of truth, which is the whole point of the cluster-side partition
  table and what G2 enables.

  Subtleties to confirm with you:
  1. Single-node fallback: when mode: standalone (no cluster), empty partitions should mean "all partitions of this topic" (read via Kafka metadata) —
  agree?
  2. Explicit list still wins: an explicit partitions: [3, 7, 11] overrides the cluster-aware default — useful for testing and for "I want this pipeline
  pinned to certain partitions" scenarios — agree?
  3. Build-time vs runtime: G1 only handles startup defaulting (read once at source build). Runtime re-assign on G2 transfer is the separate task we're
  deferring.
  4. Kafka-only or all PULL connectors? Same logic should eventually apply to JDBC scan, S3 list-fetch, etc. but for Phase 1 scope: Kafka only, design the
  cluster-handle injection so other PULL connectors can adopt it later.

  → Confirm A with subtleties 1+2+3+4 as I proposed, or pick a different combination?

  #59 — T2 scale "1→3→5": three pragmatic paths

  Quick clarifications on what scaling means:
  - HPA scales pod count by CPU/memory metrics. New pods come up but don't auto-join Raft — that's CL-5 (still parked). HPA is "elastic data plane" not
  "elastic cluster membership."
  - Raft membership change is what actually grows the cluster. Today it's manual via aeon cluster join CLI. CL-5 was going to make this auto on pod startup.

  Three paths for Phase 3:

  ┌─────┬────────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  #  │                  Path                  │                          What we test                           │          Cost (DOKS time)          │
  ├─────┼────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ A   │ 3→5 scale-up only (skip 1→3)           │ Add-Member RPC, partition rebalance to new nodes. Cluster born  │ Cheap — single helm upgrade        │
  │     │                                        │ at 3, never tested at 1.                                        │                                    │
  ├─────┼────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ B   │ 1→3→5 staged within session            │ Cluster bootstrap, single-node mode → multi-node transition,    │ Medium — two helm upgrades, two    │
  │     │                                        │ then expansion.                                                 │ re-bootstraps                      │
  ├─────┼────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────────┤
  │ C   │ A + a separate single-node soak        │ Best of both worlds, but two clusters means two provisioning    │ Most expensive                     │
  │     │ (different cluster)                    │ cycles.                                                         │                                    │
  └─────┴────────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────────────┘

  Concern with B: moving from replicas:1 → 3 means converting a single-node Raft (committed log) into a 3-node cluster. The Add-Member protocol handles this
   in openraft, but it's an exercise we haven't validated in production-shape. Could surface a fresh class of bugs that has nothing to do with what we're
  trying to test (G2 partition handover).

  My pick: A for Phase 3 — keeps focus on G2 validation. Add B as a future "membership-change validation" session (could run on local Rancher Desktop, $0).

  #60 — T6 chaos appetite menu

  Three pragmatic chaos options, increasing severity:

  ┌───────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┬────────────┐
  │               #               │                              Chaos                              │           Aeon should...            │ Pragmatic? │
  ├───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┼────────────┤
  │ PodChaos kill-one every 60 s  │ Replace pod, rejoin Raft via state snapshot, partition handover │ ✅ Solid baseline test; exercises   │            │
  │ during 10-min run             │  triggers via G2 driver. Zero event loss expected.              │ shutdown path + recovery path       │            │
  ├───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┼────────────┤
  │ NetworkChaos partition for 30 │ Quorum side stays leader+writable, minority side becomes        │                                     │            │
  │  s at minute 5                │ read-only follower, both reconverge on heal. (This is           │ ✅ Catches split-brain regressions  │            │
  │                               │ essentially T5 inside T6.)                                      │                                     │            │
  ├───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┼────────────┤
  │ NetworkChaos delay +100 ms on │ Tolerate without leader churn (raft_timing config has heartbeat │ ⚠️ Edge case — only catches         │            │
  │  QUIC port for 2 min          │  100ms / election 500-1000ms by default).                       │ misconfigured timing, low           │            │
  │                               │                                                                 │ information value                   │            │
  ├───────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┼────────────┤
  │ NodeChaos cordon-and-evict    │ Same outcome as drain test, redundant with T3                   │ ❌ Skip                             │            │
  └───────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────┴────────────┘

  My pick for T6: PodChaos kill-one + NetworkChaos partition (skip the others). Total chaos events in 10 min: ~9 pod-kills + 1 partition-heal cycle.
  Aggressive enough to be a real soak, not so chaotic that we can't attribute failures.

  → Agree, or want a different mix?

  Tell me

  1. G1 option A with the four subtleties — agree?
  2. T2 = "3→5 only" for Phase 3 — agree?
  3. T6 = PodChaos kill-one + NetworkChaos partition — agree?

  Once you confirm those three, I'll update task descriptions to match (and add the two new tasks for dynamic re-assign + status --watch) and we can start
  on #48.

 P1.1c complete — all 3 partition_driver tests green (happy path, bulk-failure abort, dedup rejection). ROADMAP row flipped to ✅ landed.

  Moving to #51 — P1.1d (G1): cluster-ownership-aware Kafka source partition defaulting. Let me find the Kafka source and the G1 context.


 Given the scope here, let me pause and check in with you on direction before I start stitching code across multiple crates.

  The G1 symptom: KafkaSourceFactory at connectors.rs:116-120 hard-defaults an empty partitions list to [0], causing silent under-read in Session A when a
  node should have been reading its owned slice of the topic.

  The plumbing choice: the factory currently gets only a SourceConfig — it has no handle to SharedClusterState, no pipeline name, no node ID. So fixing G1
  means plumbing cluster ownership somewhere that can reach the factory (or the call site). My proposed shape:

  1. New async trait PartitionOwnershipResolver in aeon-engine/src/connector_registry.rs — fn owned_partitions() -> Future<Vec<u16>>. Keyless (single
  pipeline per node today; multi-pipeline is P5).
  2. Supervisor integration: PipelineSupervisor gets an optional resolver (via OnceLock so it can be late-installed after cluster bootstrap). In start(), if
   source_cfg.partitions.is_empty() and a resolver is set, clone-and-fill the config before handing to the factory.
  3. Cluster-backed impl in a new aeon-engine/src/partition_ownership.rs (behind cluster feature) — reads SharedClusterState + installed my_id.
  4. Wire it up in cmd_serve post-bootstrap_multi.
  5. Factory change: keep the vec![0] fallback behind a tracing::warn! so single-node tests/benches don't break; the fix happens one layer up.
  6. Tests: stub resolver in supervisor tests + an integration check on the cluster-backed impl.

  Tradeoff: it's a small cross-crate refactor (4 files touched in production code + tests), but the alternative (factory directly holds SharedClusterState)
  leaks cluster types into every connector factory. Preferring the trait keeps factories pipeline/cluster-agnostic.

  Want me to go with this, or would you rather I (a) skip the trait and just wire SharedClusterState directly into KafkaSourceFactory, or (b) take a
  different angle I'm missing?

✻ Brewed for 25m 43s · 1 shell still running

===========
 I prefer interface/trait approach
 ==========
 claude's response:
 
  P1.1d complete. Summary of what landed:

  - New trait PartitionOwnershipResolver in aeon-engine/src/connector_registry.rs — async, returns Option<Vec<u16>> (None = "no ownership info," so the
  supervisor falls through to the factory's own fallback).
  - Supervisor integration — OnceLock<Arc<dyn Resolver>> field + set_ownership_resolver() setter; in start(), empty partitions lists get filled from the
  resolver before the factory sees the config.
  - Cluster-backed impl ClusterPartitionOwnership in a new aeon-engine/src/partition_ownership.rs (feature-gated cluster) — reads
  SharedClusterState.partition_table.partitions_for_node(my_id).

===========
 for  P2.2a — DOKS aeon-pool 3→5 expansion script, while you mentioned 3 to 5 nodes in DOKS cluster, do I setup 3 nodes cluster initially and then 5 nodes
   cluster or are we planning HPA usage, so we start with single-node cluster to multi-node cluster and back?

===========
claude's response:

 Clear now. Two separate dimensions — node pool (VMs) vs StatefulSet replicas (Aeon pods):

  Single DOKS cluster, lifecycle within the test session:

  1. DOKS node pool aeon-pool — VMs, provisioned via doctl. Starts at 3 (standard Session A bootstrap per deploy/doks/README.md). P2.2a is the script that
  resizes this pool from 3 → 5 via doctl k8s cluster node-pool resize (and later 5 → 3 for T3).
  2. Aeon StatefulSet replicas — pods, scaled via kubectl scale. T2 walks 1 → 3 → 5. T3 reverses 5 → 3 → 1.

  So the T2 sequence in run-t2-scaleup.sh (P2.2d) is:
  - Start: pool=3 VMs, replicas=1 (single-node Aeon on a 3-node K8s cluster — plenty of headroom)
  - kubectl scale sts/aeon --replicas=3 (fits in existing 3-node pool)
  - Call P2.2a: resize pool 3→5 (new VMs come up)
  - kubectl scale sts/aeon --replicas=5

  No HPA. Aeon is a StatefulSet (stateful Raft members); HPA doesn't fit and would conflict with manual partition handover. The "auto-scale via HPA" memory
  is explicitly tagged Future — post-Gate-2 research, not this phase.

  Want me to proceed with P2.2a as a doctl-based resize script (idempotent, takes --cluster <id> + target count, polls until VMs are Ready)?

  1. Real L2SegmentInstaller that writes incoming manifests/chunks into the local L2 body store (the EO-2 aeon_engine::l2_body module) and verifies CRC32 on
   close.

  so aeon-cli stays thin).

  Two questions before I start coding G11.a:

  1. PoH chain install: do you want the target node to re-verify the incoming PoH segment (signature + hash linkage) before installing, or trust-and-extend
  since it came over mTLS QUIC from a voting Raft peer? Re-verification is safer but doubles cutover CPU cost. I'd default to
  verify-once-per-segment-manifest (not per-entry) and make it a config flag.
  2. L2 segment install concurrency: OK to keep the install serialised per partition (single-writer into the segmented log) and parallelise across different
   partitions? The existing L2BodyStore is partition-scoped so this falls out naturally.

✻ Cooked for 3m 3s · 1 shell still running

=========
 proceed as recommended but do keep the option configurable in POH chain install feature. Proceed as suggested for L2 segment install concurrency. I have
  seen CRC32 somewhere in the previous messages. Which algorithms are we using? I remember about SHA512 for hash generation and ED25519 for signature. We
  are using merkletree in the implementation for cryptographic verifiability of the chain know. Can you please check and confirm, if all are connecting
  correctly

=========
claude's response:

 Good catch on the crypto algorithms — worth verifying before I build an installer that touches any of them. Let me survey what's actually wired.

 Crypto stack audit — here's what's already coherent, and the one real gap:

  Coherent (no action needed):
  - SHA-512 consistently — PoH chain-link (SHA-512(prev_hash ‖ merkle_root ‖ ts)), Merkle leaves + internal nodes + pair merges, MMR peaks, PoH aggregate
  checkpoint. All via sha2 v0.10. crates/aeon-crypto/src/{poh.rs, merkle.rs, mmr.rs}.
  - Ed25519 per batch (ed25519-dalek v2) — signs the Merkle root (not every PoH entry), carried in PohEntry.signed_root: Option<SignedRoot>. Chain integrity
   itself is via hash-linkage; the signature authenticates that a specific quorum-trusted key attested this root.
  - CRC32 correctly scoped — crc32fast used only for non-crypto integrity: L2 body records, L2 .l2b whole-file manifest CRC in CL-6a, batch wire format, WAL
   records. Not on any chain link.
  - Merkle is hand-rolled with SHA-512 throughout — leaf = SHA-512(event_bytes), internal = SHA-512(left ‖ right). Proofs verify by recomputing root.
  - Partition transfer L2 side (CL-6a) — already CRC32-verifies on receive (l2_transfer.rs:299-305). Fine as-is.

  The gap — PoH chain import (CL-6b):
  - PohChain::from_state(PohChainState) at aeon-crypto/src/poh.rs:306-315 deserializes and resumes trust-and-extend — no hash-chain walk, no signature
  check, no MMR consistency check. recent_entries is reset to empty so even verify_recent() post-install would be a no-op.
  - The cluster transport layer (transport/poh_transfer.rs) is deliberately crypto-agnostic — it ships opaque bincode bytes. Verification is supposed to
  live in the installer, and right now the only installer is a test-only Recording* that doesn't verify.

  Refined plan for G11.a — PoH chain install with configurable verification:

  1. aeon-crypto — add PohChain::verify_state(&PohChainState, expected_pubkey: Option<&VerifyingKey>) -> Result<(), PohVerifyError>:
    - Walks entries if MMR or any entry ring is present; recomputes SHA-512(prev_hash ‖ merkle_root ‖ ts) for each.
    - If signed_root.is_some(), verifies Ed25519 against expected_pubkey (or against signed_root.public_key if we trust-key-on-first-use; make a mode flag).
    - Re-verifies MMR peak structure (pairwise SHA-512 up the mountains).
  2. aeon-cluster — new production PohChainInstallerImpl { chain: Arc<RwLock<PohChain>>, verify_mode: PohVerifyMode } with PohVerifyMode::{Verify,
  VerifyWithKey(VerifyingKey), TrustExtend}. Default Verify. Wires via config (helm values + YAML).
  3. aeon-cluster — new production L2SegmentInstallerImpl { store: Arc<L2BodyStore> } — opens destination L2 partition dir, writes incoming chunks in order,
   confirms CRC32 on EndFrame, atomically renames .l2b.part → .l2b. Serial per partition, parallel across partitions (matches L2BodyStore invariants).
  4. Unit tests for each installer (happy path, tampered byte → verify fail, CRC mismatch → install rejected).

  Then G11.b spawns PartitionTransferDriver in ClusterNode startup, calls driver.notify() from the REST proposer, and a real-installer E2E.

  Two sub-decisions I'd like you to confirm before coding:

  1. Default verify mode — Verify (cheapest safe default: re-hash chain + check signature against whatever signed_root.public_key is carried,
  trust-key-on-first-use), or strict VerifyWithKey(cluster_pubkey) (requires cluster to have a shared attestation key configured — more work, stronger)? I'd
   pick Verify as default with VerifyWithKey available behind config for production/FIPS paths.
  2. Where does the verify config live? Two options:
    - (a) values-aeon.yaml → helm env → engine config (new field cluster.poh_verify_mode).
    - (b) Just a code constant for now; add config later.

  I'd pick (a) — adds one field and one YAML row, avoids a second touch when we need to flip it in Session B.

  OK with this shape + those defaults?

  tests green. Next action is G11.b: spawn PartitionTransferDriver::watch_loop in ClusterNode bootstrap and wire driver.notify() from the REST proposer.

=========
why  VerifyWithKey currently degrades to Verify in G11.a?

let us quickly check this and then proceed to next in the to do list
=========
claude's response:

 That confirms it. Here's the concrete reason:

  The PohChainState wire struct carries MMR-only state — no entries.

  pub struct PohChainState {
      pub partition: PartitionId,
      pub current_hash: Hash512,
      pub sequence: u64,
      pub mmr: MerkleMountainRange,  // ← root-only aggregate
  }

  recent_entries is a local field on PohChain (line 81, Vec<PohEntry>), deliberately excluded from the exported state. So the receiving node sees only a
  sequence number, a tip hash, and the MMR peaks.

  What VerifyWithKey would check if entries were present:
  1. Each PohEntry's Ed25519 signature against the sender's pubkey.
  2. Hash-chain linkage: entry[i].hash == SHA512(entry[i-1].hash || entry[i].payload || entry[i].sequence).
  3. That the chain of entries actually produces the claimed current_hash tip.

  Without entries in the wire state, there is nothing to run Ed25519 verify() against, and nothing to relink. The MMR root alone is a commitment — you can't
   reconstruct preimages from it.

  What Verify actually does today — structural/self-consistency only:
  - partition matches expected.
  - (sequence, current_hash) pair is consistent (genesis iff seq==0).
  - mmr.leaf_count() ≤ sequence.
  - mmr.peaks().len() == leaf_count.count_ones() (MMR peak arithmetic invariant).

  To make VerifyWithKey real, the fix is to extend PohChainState to carry a bounded tail of recent_entries (say N=1024) plus the sender's signing pubkey.
  The receiver would verify signatures + linkage on that tail and check its last entry's hash equals current_hash. This is a wire-format change —
  deliberately parked as a future extension so G11.a stays scoped to unblocking T4.

  Proceeding to next pending item. Per the Session A re-run priority queue in roadmap — G10 > G11 > G14 > G9 > G8 — G11 is now at G11.b. Next is task #77
  (G11.b): spawn PartitionTransferDriver::watch_loop in ClusterNode bootstrap and wire driver.notify() from the REST proposer. Want me to start on that?

==========
proceed
=========

  Code

  getter. propose_partition_transfer now calls driver.notify() right after a BeginTransfer proposal commits (ClusterResponse::Ok branch).
  - aeon-cli/src/main.rs — after install_registry_applier + ownership resolver, constructs L2SegmentInstaller + PohChainInstallerImpl + RaftNodeResolver +
  PartitionTransferDriver, installs on node, spawns watch_loop using node.shutdown_flag() so it dies with the node. Gated on AEON_PIPELINE_NAME — unset =
  log warning + skip (single-node / pre-configured runs still boot).
  - Helm: values.yaml gained cluster.pipelineName and cluster.l2Root; statefulset.yaml emits AEON_PIPELINE_NAME and AEON_L2_ROOT when set.

   but the "PoH chain has no gaps across transfers" Gate 2 row can't pass. Task #78 created.

 Let me audit the existing source connectors first — you're right that I shouldn't be proposing a new push source if one already exists.

 You're right — I had amnesia on that. The connector ecosystem already covers both methods cleanly:

  Push (server-side, inbound): http/webhook_source (axum POST + push_buffer backpressure, 202/503 semantics), websocket/source, quic/source,
  webtransport/source + datagram variant, mqtt/source.
  Pull (client-side, outbound): kafka/source, http/polling_source, file, postgres_cdc/mysql_cdc/mongodb_cdc, redis_streams, nats, rabbitmq.

  Shared crates/aeon-connectors/src/push_buffer.rs gives all push sources a consistent three-phase backpressure contract. So V4 is not new code — it's
  validating an existing push source end-to-end through the Aeon pipeline, same shape as the Kafka pull path we already run.
